And since we’ve already discussed (2), we can move on to (4). (4) is obviously a non-starter. But, for what extremely little it’s worth, here’s the argument behind it. Scientific hypotheses are supposed to deal with things that can be publicly verified. Suppose that scientist Smith insisted that he, and he alone, were able to see (or otherwise sense-perceive) the data that confirmed some hypothesis of his. Obviously Smith wouldn’t be taken seriously. My pains, tickles, etc., cannot be witnessed by anyone other than myself. For this reason, some psychologists who were attempting to scientize their discipline said that such things had no place within science and therefore had no place in a scientific world-view. Given this position, it is a short step to saying that pains, tickles, etc., don’t exist at all. In a word, science deals with what is objective; mental states are subjective; therefore, mental states don’t exist. There are many fallacies in this argument. One of them is that, in actuality, the existence of pains, tickles, etc., is...

positions.title: MMSE-112 Position
positions.thesis: The general definition of behaviorism is this. If M is any mental category, P is an instance of M just in case P is a disposition on the part of some individual to respond with certain overt behaviors to certain sensory inputs. Behaviorism is false. How a person reacts to a given sensory input is a function, not only of what that input is, but also of what that person’s existing mental states are. If you don’t speak English, an utterance of “what is 1 + 1?” will not cause you to say “2.” It is only in people who have various pieces of background knowledge (e.g., knowledge of linguistic conventions) that such a disposition corresponds to the knowledge that 1 + 1 = 2. What we said about that particular disposition is true (mutatis mutandis) of any other. By itself, no disposition to engage in a certain kind of overt behavior, given certain stimuli, corresponds to any mental state: what does so is such a disposition plus various pre-existing mental states. So, mental states cannot be reduced to behavioral dispositions. At most, they can be reduced to behavioral dispositions plus various mental states. But this means that they can’t be reduced to behavioral dispositions. Aware of these problems with behaviorism, but still wanting to holding onto the idea that mental entities could be dispositionalized (identified with tendencies to respond with certain overt behaviors to certain inputs), many philosophers of mind responded to this by adopting functionalism, which we’ve already discussed and will now discuss further.

positions.title: MMSE-113 Position
positions.thesis: what I'm saying might be false, even though it has that same syntax as (4) "n is identical with n." If our thinking were syntax-driven, we couldn't recognize the differences in respect of logical form between (1) and (2) or between (3) and (4). We can recognize those differences. So our thinking is not syntax-driven. So CTM is wrong. Here is a similar line of thought. If, on July 1, 2010, in Richmond, VA, I say: (i) "right now, Jim is three thousand miles from here," the information encoded in my utterance is very different that encoded in an utterances of: (ii) "On July 1, 2010, Jim is 3,000 miles from Richmond VA." The information encoded in (i) doesn't follow from the information encoded in (ii), or vice versa. In general, when the context-sensitive expressions in a true statement are replaced with context-insensitive expressions, the result is a statement that, although true, neither entails the first statement nor follows from it. For an expression to be context-sensitive is for its referent to vary with the context. What an expression refers to is determined by its semantics, not its syntax. CTM says that thought is to no degree semantics-driven. If that were true, we couldn't grasp perspectival truths, such as that expressed by (i). Since we can grasp such truths, CTM is false. 5.0 CTM’s reliance on the erroneous assumption that there are minimal units of thought First a definition: a “morpheme” is an expression that cannot be decomposed into others. Bearing this in mind, let’s suppose that, just as CTM says, we think in a language. In that case, our concepts are identical with expressions. Linguistic expressions consist of morphemes. (Any given complex expression; e.g., “the cat is on the mat,” decomposes into expressions that cannot, in their turn, be further decomposed; (e.g., “cat,” “mat”). So in contending that our thoughts are identical with (tokens of) Mentalese symbols, CTM is, in effect, assuming that we have simple concepts: concepts that cannot be decomposed in others. So CTM, if correct, entails that any given thought that one has can be decomposed into “simple” concepts. CTM thus has the radically implausible consequence that we have “simple” concepts. In other words, CTM has the consequence that some of our concepts are no more capable of being decomposed into other concepts than a morpheme (e.g., the word “cat”) can be decomposed into other expressions....

positions.title: MMSE-114 Position
positions.thesis: The argument just given, though accepted by many, involves a gross overestimation of what our linguistic practices can give us in the way of philosophical illumination.[418] It’s true that we refer to certain (brain)dead objects as “people.” But, it seems to me, that could well just be a verbal convention. We refer to scooters of a certain kind as “rascals.” But they’re not really rascals. (A rascal is a mischievous young person.) I also think that our tendency to describe (brain-)dead bodies as “people” has emotional roots. At some level, we are holding onto the idea that, deep within that ravaged body, is a mind. In any case, the present author puts no stock in arguments such as (1)–(4). 4.1 A better argument for the physicalistic view But there is a much better argument for the physicalistic conception of personal identity. A story will help us state this argument. Smith is a normal adult with a vigorous mind. One day he’s hit by a bus. There is a complete cessation of all mental activity. Smith’s stream of mental activity has been snipped. But Smith’s body doesn’t die. It goes into a vegetative coma for 20 years. During that time, there is no mental activity at all in what was once Smith’s brain. The brain centers regulating automatic functions (e.g., respiration, digestion) work just fine, but the parts involved in mental activity are totally inactive. No thoughts, no dreams, no sensations. Nothing. After 20 years, the body wakes up, and the hitherto inactive brain centers spring back to life. The occupant of that body is obviously in complete command of his faculties. His personality traits are just like those of the pre-accident occupant of that same body, as are the values, the memories, the cognitive aptitudes, and so on. Let X be the person who occupied that body before the accident and let Y be the person who’s been occupying it since the accident. Is X the same person as Y? Is the post-coma person Smith? Or is it somebody who is psychologically a lot like Smith, but isn’t actually him? Surely it really is Smith. That’s what we’d say, anyway. It’s very hard to believe that Y is a brand-new person (albeit one who has the psychological characteristics ordinarily had only by adults). This is a serious problem for the mentalistic view of personal identity. According to the mentalist, Smith...

positions.title: MMSE-115 Position
positions.thesis: I sent a response to Dr. McMullin in which I responded to both questions and which, because it expresses my current views, I will quote verbatim: The brain-structures that give rise to fleeting mental occurrences, and that constitute the self, do consist of swarms of events; but those structures don't consist of mental events. Consider, for example, your ability to speak English. Presumably, that ability is constituted by some brain-structure. The neurons (or whatever) that, in their turn, constitute that structure are, in their turn, constituted by (I'm skipping some intermediate levels of description) minute displacements of mass-energy. Those displacements are not themselves mental, even though, ultimately, they're mind-constitutive. (No contradiction here: the atoms that compose cells are not themselves biological entities, even though those cells are such entities.) In connection with your question about mental structures, you make an important point that, if correct, undermines much of what I say but that, I will now try show, is not correct. (In what follows, I’ll use the word “thing” in the narrowest possible sense. So, as I’ll be using it, rocks, apples, and trees are “things,” but neither non-spatiotemporal entities, e.g. possibilities, nor spatiotemporal but intangible entities, e.g. gravitational fields, qualify as “things.”) Just as you say, my position is that events are the ultimate constituents of the spatiotemporal world and, therefore, that: (i) Although there could be events in the absence of things, there couldn't be things in the absence of events. Your position is that things (substances), not events, are the ultimate constituents of the spatiotemporal world and, therefore, that: (ii) Although there could be things in the absence of events, there couldn't be events in the absence of events. The first question to ask is: what do you believe “things” to be? There are three possibilities: (a) Things consist entirely of property-instances. Consider the rock’s weight, shape, color, etc. There is nothing underlying those property instances; there are just the property instances.

positions.title: MMSE-116 Position
positions.thesis: For argument’s sake, let’s suppose that either (b) or (c) is correct. In that case, underlying a given rock’s shape, mass, color, position, etc., there is something that has no shape, mass, color, etc., i.e. there is something that, although it underlies properties, is itself featureless. A featureless thing is a surd. Nothing can have no properties. Thus, both (b) and (c) are false. So (a) must correct. But if (a) is right, then events are the most basic constituents of spatiotemporal world. Here’s why. First of all, for an event to occur is for some property to either come to be instantiated or to cease to be instantiated. (In this context, “property” is short for “property or multiplicity of properties.”) There would be no events if there were no changes, and there would be no changes if there were no cases of properties coming or ceasing to be instantiated. Further, there would be no passage of time if there were no events. To say that it's been a year since you've seen Smith is to say that certain periodic processes have happened a certain number of times since you last saw Smith. Thus, period during which absolutely nothing happens cannot possibly last for more than an instant. It follows that any case of a property-instance's existing for a non-null period of time is parasitic on cases of change and, therefore, on events. Since, as we’ve established, “things” are wholly constituted by property-instances, it follows that any case of a thing’s enduring for more than instant is parasitic on the existence of change. The concept of a thing that exists for no more than an instant is incoherent. Things necessarily consist of property-instances, as we’ve seen. So a thing’s existing for a mere instant would involve there being some property P such that, within a time-interval of zero duration, P came to be and also ceased to be instantiated. Those events, involving as they do the very same property-instance, cannot possibly be simultaneous. So the sequence consisting of those two events would necessarily occupy a time-interval that was greater than an instant. Thus, there could not possibly be a thing that existed for just one instant. Since, as we saw a moment ago, any case of a thing’s enduring for more than instant is parasitic on the existence of change, it follows that changes, or events, are the most fundamental...

positions.title: MMSE-117 Position
positions.thesis: In my view, I can shake hands with people, and also see them, play tennis with them, and otherwise interact with them. I thus reject 4-D. 5.1.3.1 Four-dimensionalism (continued) Things change. 4-D denies this. If 4-D is right, the thing that is reading this sentence right now isn’t you; it’s just some time-slice of you. And the slightly different thing that was reading the first sentence of this chapter isn’t you either; it’s just another time-slice of you. The same is true of the thing that decided to buy this book a few weeks ago. According to 4-D, although the thing reading this sentence is different in some ways from the thing that was reading the first sentence of this chapter, those differences aren’t expressions of the fact that some one thing has changed. According to 4-D, you don’t change. For a given object to change is for the way that object is at one time to be different from the way it is at some other time. According to 4­D, for any time t, what exists at t isn’t an object, and is only some “time-slice” of an object. Therefore, there are no two moments of time t and t* such that how some one object is at t is different from how that same object is at t*. Therefore, if 4-D is right, things don’t change. But this is absurd. Things change. I have changed, and so have you. And each of us will continue to change. 4-D is either dead wrong or it amounts to nothing more than a proposal that we use the words “thing” and “change” in non-standard ways. And even if by some remote chance it turns out that things don’t change, it would be bad methodology to make our theory of personal identity depend on that turning out to be the case. In doing so, we’d be assuming facts (or non-facts, rather) that aren’t yet in evidence. Assuming, as we probably must, that things change, the only way to go is to say that identity, in the diachronic sense, is to be understood in terms of relations of causal connectedness—that statements like “Bill is the guy in the photo who is wearing a rugby shirt” are to be analyzed in the way described earlier. Since the relevant sort of connectedness obviously comes in degrees, so does identity. It should be pointed out that...

positions.title: MMSE-118 Position
positions.thesis: There are two ways to interpret this. On the one hand, Hume could be saying that selves don’t exist. On the other, he could be saying that selves are constituted by the thoughts, feelings, etc., that we describe them as “having.” The right interpretation is the second one. Notice how frequently the word “I” occurs in his argument. He obviously believes that, when he uses that word, he's referring to himself and thus to his self. And surely he's right about this. It seems a truism that, if they refer to anything, tokens of "I" refer to selves; and it also seems a truism that tokens of "I" do refer. Also, in other parts of the Treatise, Hume talks at length about the relation that two different mental contents must bear to each other to belong to the same mind or self. Thus, Hume’s position is that, although selves do exist, they are no more distinct from the mental events that occupy them than a swarm of wasps exists independently of the wasps that compose it. For an event to occupy a mind is for it to constitute it. The just-quoted argument, it will be noticed, is empirical. Hume is arguing on observational, as opposed to strictly logical or conceptual, grounds that there isn’t anything that underlies his various thoughts. When he looks inward, he finds thoughts and nothing else; in particular, he doesn’t find a non-thought that “supports” these various thoughts. This fact is important, as we’ll now see. 6.1.3 Problems with Hume’s argument Given only that we can’t observe something, it doesn’t follow that it doesn’t exist. There are many unobservable entities (e.g., electrons). If Freud (1915) is right, there are unobservable (unintrospectible) mental entities (e.g., unconscious urges). Saying that selves don’t exist since we can’t introspect them is like saying that electrons don’t exist since we can’t see them. A related possibility that Hume is ignoring is that minds are structures of the kind described earlier. (This oversight is probably deliberate, as we’ll see in a moment.) He’s also ignoring the correlative possibility that specific mental occurrences (e.g., images, tickles) are products of minds, and are not themselves constitutive of them. In ignoring these possibilities, Hume is stacking the deck in his favor. We are pretheoretically inclined to see minds as being to specific tickles, itches etc., what car factories are to cars. In other words, we’re...

positions.title: MMSE-119 Position
positions.thesis: This argument, unlike the first, isn’t empirical at all. This time, Hume is trying to show on purely conceptual grounds that there is nothing underlying the various events occupying a person’s mind. He’s arguing that the very idea of such a thing is an incoherent one, like that of a square circle. Why is this idea incoherent? Because, says Hume, it has the absurd consequence that there could be thought-free minds. 6.2.2 Evaluating this argument: Hume’s failure to distinguish between two very different meanings of the word “thought” Hume is right that if there is some inner flame underlying one’s thoughts, and therefore distinct from them, it does follow that one’s self could exist in a thought-free condition. But Hume is wrong to say that a thought-free self is an absurdity. The reason Hume thinks it absurd is that he fails to distinguish two very different meanings of the word “thought.” Sometimes the word “thought” refers to mental occurrences—to what are now known as occurrent mental phenomena. An occurrent mental phenomenon is anything mental that happens. Sense-perceptions are occurrent mental phenomena; and so are acts of ratiocination and emotional epiphanies. (This list is far from complete.) But sometimes the word “thought” refers to the relatively stable structures that underlie mental occurrences. “For the first twenty years of my academic career,” a philosopher once said to me, “I thought that Platonism was false.” This person was describing a stable, structural constituent of his mind, not a mental happening. If, by “thought” Hume means “mental happening” then, contrary to what he alleges, there’s nothing incoherent in the idea of a mind that, during some period, isn’t populated by thoughts. If by “thought” Hume means “stable mental structure” then Hume is right to say that there couldn’t be thought-free minds. Earlier, we saw why this is so. If selves were distinct from their thoughts, there could be thought-free minds. So, given that there cannot be thought-free minds, selves are constituted by the thoughts that belong to them. So, after a fashion, Hume is right. Just as he says, there is nothing to one’s self other than the thoughts one has. But this claim of his isn’t right in the way he thought it was. He thought that one’s self consists of the thoughts (i.e., fleeting mental occurrences) that occupy it, and not of the thoughts (i.e., stable structures that create and regulate such...

positions.title: MMSE-120 Position
positions.thesis: (c) One can travel backwards in time. It is generally thought that (a)–(c) are empirical questions that fall within the jurisdiction of physics. In actuality, they are questions of logic, not of empirical fact. Moreover, each of (a)–(c) is logically false and therefore couldn’t possibly be correct. Let’s discuss (a) first. In this chapter, we have seen that anything that persists for any amount of time is a causal series. A causal series is a continuous series of states of affairs such that, given any two of them, one is the (in)direct cause of the other. An illustration may be helpful. At any given moment during its existence, your car consists of an incessant flurry of mass-energy displacements; and your car is thus a series of “event-flurries,” as we’ll call them. Twenty-four hours ago, your car was constituted by one event-flurry. Right now it’s constituted by a different event-flurry. (Let S1 be the first event-flurry and let S2 be the second.) And, of course, my car is currently constituted by some event-flurry. (Let S3 be that flurry.) Bearing all of this in mind, imagine the following. While your car was parked in your driveway, somebody crashed into it, severely damaging it. Your car therefore bears little resemblance to what it was twenty-four hours ago. It follows that S1 and S2 are qualitatively very different from each other. By contrast, S1 and S3 are as similar to each other as their being in different places permits. This is sheer coincidence: S1 and S3 are, we will suppose, as causally disconnected from each other as any two non-simultaneous events on this planet can be. It follows that my car, as it is right now, is just like your car, as it was twenty-four hours ago, before it was damaged. It also follows that S1 is much less similar to S2 than it is to S3. Be all of this as it may, the car in your driveway is, whereas the car in my driveway is not, numerically identical with the car you owned twenty-four hours ago. Mediating between S1 and S2 is a continuous series of events such that, given any two of them, one is the (in)direct cause of the other. No such series mediates between S1 and S3. That’s why (i) the car in my driveway isn’t the very same car as the one you parked in your driveway twenty-four...

positions.title: MMSE-121 Position
positions.thesis: So depending on whether he’s somewhere or nowhere, Smith is either permanently annihilated in 2009 or he’s no more time-traveling than anything else that exists during that period. Therefore, (i) is false. To see why (ii) is false we need do little more than repeat some of the points just made. Suppose that Smith travels from 2009 to 2023 at a rate of one million minutes/minute. We’ve just seen that Smith cannot make this journey in a discontinuous manner; he cannot disappear from one time and reappear at some other, non-adjacent time. So Smith must make this journey in a continuous manner. So at any given moment during this interval, Smith must be in some place or other. It follows, as we saw a moment ago, that he is no more time-traveling than anything else that exists during that interval. The supposition that Smith is traveling forward in time at a rate of one million minutes/minute thus turns out to collapse into the supposition that he isn’t time-traveling at all. Given any positive number n, an analogous argument shows that continuous forward time-travel at a rate of n minutes/minute collapses into forward time-travel at a rate of one minute/minute. There is another, quite different problem with the concept of discontinuous time-travel. Let W1 be a universe where (i) is true and (ii) is false; and let W2 be an otherwise indistinguishable universe where (ii) is true and (i) is false. What happens in the one universe at any given time coincides with what happens in the other universe at that time. It follows that, even if (per impossibile) an occupant of the one universe knew exactly what happened in that universe at any given time, he would not, in virtue of that fact, have any legitimate reason for preferring (i) to (ii). A fortiori, no possible observation that one might have would provide one with any reason for believing oneself to be in W1 as opposed to W2. Since observation couldn’t possibly provide one with such a reason, only logic could do so. But logic cannot do so, since (i) is logically false, as we’ve already seen.[432]

positions.title: MMSE-122 Position
positions.thesis: Similar points hold in connection with discontinuous backwards time­ travel. In the movie Back to the Future, young Marty McFly vanishes from the year 1985 and shows up in the year 1955. This means that, in the year 1985, the causal sequence that constitutes his existence is severed. Like everything else that persists for any length of time, Marty McFly is a causal sequence. It follows that, so far as it isn’t incoherent, the statement: (1) In 1985, Marty McFly traveled backwards in time to the year 1955 and is otherwise unchanged. collapses into: (2) Marty McFly was annihilated in 1985, and in 1955 there appears ex nihilo somebody who is numerically distinct from him but otherwise just like him. Also, given obvious adaptations of what we said a moment ago, there is no conceivable event that, were it known, would provide more support for (1) than for (2). Since, therefore, there couldn’t possibly be any observation-based reason for preferring (1) to (2), the only possible reasons for doing so would be of a logical nature. This means that there are no reasons for doing so, given that (1) is incoherent. The concept of discontinuous backwards time-travel is therefore a broken one. What about the idea of continuous backwards time-travel? For argument’s sake, let’s suppose that, during his continuous journey from the year 1985 to the year 1955, Marty McFly is right next to some stop-sign that was built in 1920 and stays in the same place for several centuries. That means that, at any given moment during that thirty year period, Marty McFly is no more time-traveling than the stop-sign. Each is just sitting there. There are, of course, some important differences between Mary McFly and the stop-sign. First of all, whereas the stop-sign is crushed or burnt or otherwise destroyed in some pedestrian way, Marty McFly must simply vanish in the year 1985. (If he doesn’t, then he’s still hanging around in the year 1985, in which case he obviously hasn’t gone back in time.) And, whereas the stop-sign’s presence in the year 1955 is to be explained in the customary way, Marty McFly just popped up ex nihilo. Bearing these points in mind, let W3 be a universe in which: (3) Marty McFly continuously traveled backwards in time from the year 1985 to the year 1955 but is otherwise unchanged. is true, and in which (2) is...

positions.title: MMSE-123 Position
positions.thesis: We must also take into account that, at least according to some viewpoints, the choices we make have extremely deep roots in our unconscious mental lives. Analysis tends to suggest that decisions that seem, at the level of consciousness, to be “random” are, in fact, strictly determined by unconscious forces. In any case, this is one point of view, and it is one for which there is some corroboration. But suppose, if only for argument’s sake, that your choices were not fixed by the state of the universe prior to their occurrence. For the reasons given earlier, that wouldn’t make those choices be free. On the contrary, it would make them more like fits or seizures than like considered, freely made judgments. A story similar to the one just told may help make this clear. You are a student at State University. You have an A-average. All of a sudden, you choose to drop out of college and become a drug dealer. Your choice was not caused to occur by anything; the state of your personality prior to the choice had nothing to do with your making it, and neither did the state of the universe. It just happened. So, in the middle of a test, you throw down your pen, bolt out of the room, and get to work as a drug dealer. Does this sound like a freely made choice? No. It sounds more like a compulsion. It’s as though you were “taken over” by another person and lost control of your body. Again, we see that indeterminism doesn’t help freedom and actually undermines it. 4.0 The Compatibilist Conception of Freedom The problem is that, as we saw, determinism also seems to threaten freedom. Obviously you have no control over the past. If determinism is right, then what happened in the past completely determines exactly what’s happening in the present and, in particular, completely fixes what choices you’re making now. So something over which you have no control is completely responsible for the choices that you’re making. It’s hard to see how, under these circumstances, you could be said to have free will. Nonetheless, some hold that there can be free will in a deterministic world. Such people are known as compatibilists. Those who deny it are incompatibilists. 4.1 The crude form of compatibilism There are ultimately two different forms of compatibilism. One of these is very straightforward;...

positions.title: MMSE-124 Position
positions.thesis: and, finally, (iii) that Chisholm’s has shown that the concept associated by the lay-person with the term “free” is incoherent and, moreover, that the same is true, though not for the same reasons, with Locke’s attempt to remove the incoherencies from that concept (while leaving it otherwise unchanged). A story will clarify these points. On Monday, you undergo hypnosis. You do so because you believe that it will help you kick your addiction to nicotine. While under hypnosis, the hypnotist, who is a prankster and loves to abuse the power he has over his subjects, tells you that, on Wednesday, you should choose to put on a tin-foil hat, go into your boss’s office, and tell him that aliens are about take over the Earth. On Wednesday, you choose to do these things. So you voluntarily put on a tin-foil hat, etc. Your act was voluntary. You chose to do it. But was it free? My opinion: there was a sense in which it was free, but there was also an important sense in which it was not free. You are not, by virtue of choosing to engage in this odd behavior, in the same category as somebody who wants to walk to his car but is physically restrained. But, for reasons that are not immediately obvious but that we will now try to elucidate, your behavior is either unfree or free only in some attenuated sense. One way of interpreting what Chisholm is saying is as follows[442]: (CR) There is only one significant difference between, on the one hand, your voluntary decision to don a tin-foil hat, etc. and, on the other hand, a ‘normal’ decision (e.g., a decision to play tennis made by somebody whose choice isn’t the result of hypnotic suggestion or any other comparable event). The difference in question is that, whereas your decision was implanted in you by some sentient being, the tennis-player’s decision was implanted in him by impersonal forces. But does that really make a difference? To be sure, your decision (to put on a tin-foil hat, etc.) is the result of malfeasance on somebody’s part, whereas the tennis-player’s decision is not. So, from a moral perspective, there are important differences between the two situations. But, when evaluating whether a person’s decision to do X is free, what is relevant is whether that person had control over the forces leading to that decision;...

positions.title: MMSE-125 Position
positions.thesis: (1) entails that, given the state of the universe at t, it was nomically impossible (i.e., prohibited by natural law) for Smith not to decide to go for a jog at t*; and (2) also entails this. But there is a difference between (1) and (2). (1) says that the state of the universe at t compelled Smith to make a certain decision. (2) doesn’t say this. It says that it was inevitable that he would decide at t* to for a jog. But that's compatible with his not being compelled at t* to decide to go for a jog. It's inevitable that I will choose to have a meal in the next 12 hours; but that doesn't mean that my decision to eat isn't free. Rational people freely but inevitably choose to act in certain ways. Here’s a case of Smith’s being compelled to behave in a certain way. A very strong person takes hold of Smith’s arms and, despite Smith’s valiant resistance, makes Smith hit himself with his own hands (or, what is probably a more accurate way of putting it, hits Smith with Smith’s own hands). Why is it that, in this case, Smith’s behavior is the result of compulsion? Because the immediate causes of that behavior were not Smith-internal. The forces that led to Smith’s arm-movements were not constitutive of Smith; they failed to be Smith-constitutive even in the attenuated sense in which an impulse is constitutive of a person of a person who regards that impulse as having no value, having thus made every effort to dissociate himself from it, and to which he therefore gives no weight when making decisions. Even though (2) is to the effect that it is compulsory that Smith come into existence and, in due course, do such and such, (2) is quite consistent with Smith’s never being compelled in any way. For (2) is consistent with the proposition that the thing that is being compelled is the universe, not Smith, and that what the universe is being compelled to do is to produce a being, namely Smith, who will decide at t* to go jogging, and whose decision to do so, having for its proximal cause a conglomerate of Smith-constitutive forces, is not compelled---at least not in the sense in which, in the other story, Smith was compelled to hit himself and, indeed, not in any ordinary sense of...

positions.title: MMSE-126 Position
positions.thesis: But a close cousin of CFA is correct, namely: (CFA*) x caused y to occur just in case, if x hadn’t happened, then ceteris paribus y wouldn’t have happened. Similarly, even though, for the reasons just given: (PAP)[446] “x did A freely if and only if x did A and, further, had x chosen not to do A, x wouldn’t have done A,” is false, nonetheless: (PAP*) “x did A freely if and only if x did A and, further, had x chosen not to do A, then ceteris paribus x wouldn’t have done A,” is correct. It’s well known that, if they are to come out correct, counterfactuals typically have to be hedged with ceteris paribus clauses. Even though: (JFK) “If JFK hadn’t been assassinated, he would have been re-elected,” is a plausible counterfactual, it isn’t necessarily true. What if, sometime after Nov. 22, 1963, but before the ‘64 elections, JFK died of natural causes or was disgraced by some scandal? In that case, he would not have been re­elected. So the right counterfactual is: (JFK*) “If JFK hadn’t been assassinated, then ceteris paribus he would have been re-elected.” In other words, if JFK hadn’t been assassinated, but otherwise things were the same, so that JFK was healthy and popular, etc., then he would have been re-elected.[447]

positions.title: MMSE-127 Position
positions.thesis: Notice that JFK* is really a causal claim, since it’s just another way of saying: (JFK#) “JFK’s being assassinated caused an otherwise re-election-bound JFK to not be re-elected.” As a rule, counterfactuals are causal claims.[448] Thus, “Smith killed Jones freely” is true just in case (i) Smith killed Jones and (ii) if Smith had chosen not to kill Jones, then ceteris paribus Smith would have not killed Jones. Basically, Smith killed Jones freely if his doing so expressed his decision to do so, as opposed to some other force. In general: (FA)[449] an act is free if it expresses one’s decision to act that way. This is clearly the right analysis of a kind of freedom or---what may be a more accurate way of putting it---a certain aspect of freedom. 4.1.7 Freedom not a property of choices If FA is right, acts are free, not choices. This is what Locke held. Locke was the first advocate of FA, and he was an exceptionally effective one. One chooses to act, Locke said; one doesn’t choose to choose. It makes no sense to suppose that one chooses to choose. For if one always chose what to choose, Locke points out, one would have to make infinitely many choices in order to make a single one, and one therefore couldn’t make any choices at all. In order to choose to open the door, one would have to choose to choose to open the door; and in order to choose to choose to open the door, one would have to choose to choose to choose to open the door; and so on. So freedom is freedom to act, not freedom to choose. As Locke puts it, freedom is a relationship between choice and act, and not between subject and choice. It follows that, so far as one’s freedom is concerned, it is irrelevant whether one’s choices are caused or not. Since freedom is a relationship between choices and acts, not between choices and their own antecedents, it doesn’t matter whether choices are caused. Nor, therefore, does it matter how they’re caused. In fact, if FA is right, determinism is not only compatible with freedom, but is a prerequisite for it. Wanting to make the elevator come to your floor, you intend to push the button. If a degenerative neurological condition has undermined the integrity of the mechanisms linking your brain to your hand, thereby...

positions.title: MMSE-128 Position
positions.thesis: According to Locke, you are free to the extent that you can do what you want to do, and you are unfree to the extent that you cannot. This is obviously a kind of freedom—a very important kind. But it isn’t the only kind. Contrary to what Locke says, there is a sense in which choices can be free or unfree. Harry Frankfurt made this clear.[451] A story will help us begin to understand Frankfurt’s insightful analysis of freedom. Jones is a great writer who is addicted to heroin. Because his addiction is only moderate, he is able to function perfectly well even when he is heroin-deprived. (When heroin-deprived, he doesn’t feel good, but he is in no way incapacitated.) He knows that, if he were to shoot up right now, the subsequent lethargy would make it impossible for him to write a single word for the rest of the day. He also knows that, if he doesn’t write 10 pages today, he will lose his book contract. Jones does not want to lose the contract. Plus, he wants to grow as a writer, which he won’t do, at least not today, if he shoots up. He thus has a desire to not take the heroin—even though he simultaneously has a desire to take the heroin. Jones’ desire to take heroin is a first-order desire. Jones’ desire not to act on this desire is a second-order desire. This is because Jones’ desire not to take heroin is a desire as to what to do with another desire; it is a desire not to cave in to a desire to take heroin. Suppose that Jones caves in and he takes the heroin. In that case, Jones is a slave to his desires. His will has been compromised. He is not acting freely. But now suppose that Jones does not cave in. He does the right thing: instead of taking the heroin, he endures the discomfort of a heroin-free day and manages to get his work done. In this context, Jones is not a slave to his desires. His will has not been bent. His conduct expresses a “free will.” In light of considerations such as these, Frankfurt says that somebody has a free will to the extent that his conduct aligns with his second-order desires. But here a question arises. Why are second-order desires so special? Why is it that freedom...

positions.title: MMSE-129 Position
positions.thesis: Having distinguished between rational conduct and intelligent, we may proceed with our discussion of Frankfurt’s analysis of freedom. 5.01 Frankfurt’s analysis of freedom (continued) There’s more to being free than being able to do what you want to do. Your desires must reflect who you are. If they don’t, those desires belong to you but aren’t of you. And in that case, you are alienated from your own mental states, in much the way that a person with Parkinson’s is alienated from his body. But, in a way, the alienation is even worse. Involuntary behavior is unintentional behavior. Intentional behavior is voluntary behavior. Voluntary behavior is desire-driven behavior. (If you don’t want to raise your arm, but it goes up anyway, you didn’t intend for it to happen.) Epileptic fits are not actions. Unintended behaviors are not actions. Unlike actions, they’re undergone, not done. So estrangement from one’s own actions amounts to estrangement from all of one’s own desires; and it’s hard to imagine a condition less free than that. 5.1 Frankfurt’s emendation of this analysis Another story will help expose a problem with the analysis of freedom just described, and it will also clarify the raison d’être for Frankfurt’s soon to be discussed emendations of it. Smith’s first-order desires are seriously out of alignment with his second. He values writing poetry; he’s good at it; and he enjoys it about as much as it’s humanly possible to enjoy it. But he has a voracious craving for drugs. His craving for drugs, especially cocaine, is unusually strong; and his desire to write poetry, though intense, is not as intense as his desire to do cocaine. Smith’s desire to do cocaine is out of alignment with many of his values (second-order desires). Were he to give into this desire, he couldn’t write poetry; nor could he maintain friendships or play sports or, with only a few exceptions, do any of the other things he values. For this reason, he doesn’t give in. But given how intense his craving is, every day is a struggle for Smith. If, as Frankfurt suggests, one is free to the extent that one acts on one’s second-order desires, Smith is as free as a person can be. But given how hard life is for Smith, it’s counterintuitive to suppose that he’s maximally free. To be sure he has a kind of freedom, and he has it...

positions.title: MMSE-130 Position
positions.thesis: So, in deferring to the senior professor, Green isn’t acting according to his second order desires. Rather, he’s acting according to what he’s deluded himself into believing are his second order desires. (Consequently, the fact that Green’s actions are not free, except in the sense that they’re voluntary, is consistent with Frankfurt’s analysis.) (iii) Situations such as those described by GR neither constitute cases where one set of higher-order values is replaced with another nor constitute cases where some new set of higher-order values has altogether failed to have been installed. Rather, such situations involve a bifurcation or fracturing of the psyche--­a kind of splitting of oneself into two selves, one of which still has the old set of values and the other of which has the new set of values. So, according to this hypothesis, rationalizations do install new higher-order desires. Contrary to (iii), however, they do so, not by ousting old higher-order desires, but by adding new ones to them. Given that the new ones are inconsistent with the old ones, rationalization necessarily involves a degradation of one’s psychological integrity. One’s thinking must become more illogical than it used to be or one’s agency---by which I mean the totality of faculties involved in one’s being able to decide what to do---must undergo a sort of splitting. (iv) Although second-order values can be uninstalled and replaced with others, a distinction must be made between higher-order values, on the one hand, and core values, on the other. All core values are higher-order values, but not all higher-order values are core values. Green’s actions are unfree because, although they’re consistent with (some of) his higher-order values, they aren’t consistent with his core values. 2.1 Free acts = value-driven acts (i) is out of the question. To say that people don’t rationalize is itself to rationalize, given what an obvious empirical fact it is that people rationalize. Also, the essence of Frankfurt’s position is that, if one is to be free, one must not be at war with oneself: intrapsychic conflict is the enemy of freedom. Frankfurt is aware that there are different ways of eliminating intrapsychic conflict and that some of them not only fail to bring one freedom but strip one of it. For example, given somebody who is torn between shooting heroin and kicking his addiction, that person can cease to be at war with himself by relinquishing his...

positions.title: MMSE-131 Position
positions.thesis: This position is a near duplicate of a position that Freud explicitly advocated, in various forms, throughout his career.[459] It may be possible in some attenuated, strictly logical sense for there to be Intrapsychic conflict without self-deception or rationalization. But, human psychology being what it is, it seems that self-deception is essential to the perpetuation, if not the initiation, of such conflict. In any case, (i) is prima facie discrepant with empirical facts; and, although many philosophers and psychologists virulently deny (i), they do so on a priori grounds that, as we’ll see, are made of straw or, what is much more common, they don’t bother to defend their position. (ii) is inconsistent with empirical facts. People’s values do change. As a 20 year old, Jim wants to save the world. He works for the Peace Corps. He spends his weekends working at soup kitchens. As a 40 year old, Jim is an investment banker who lives to accumulate wealth and wouldn’t dream of not having a live-in maid, butler, etc. One could say that, even as a 20 year old, Jim’s values were really those of a greedy banker or that, as a 40 year old, his values are really those of a hippie do-gooder. But such a position would be ad hoc and counter-empirical. To be sure, there are many cases where somebody who is in fact a greedy sociopath works at a soup-kitchen (etc.), so as to bolster his resume in the hopes of making a lot of money on Wall St. And there are people who make a lot of money on Wall St. who wish that they were doing good for the world and whose consequent grief all the gin in the world cannot drown out. But there are also people whose values truly change. (iii) describes something that, I suspect, really does happen. But, I also suspect, it does not describe what is typically happening in cases of rationalization. In my personal experience, and in the clinical experience of many an authority, what typically happens is that some one set of higher-order desires becomes corrupt: there is a mixing of the old (pure) higher-order desires with new (corrupt, rationalization-driven) higher-order desires. There is still just one agent. There aren’t two agents—a Dr. Jekyll and a Mr. Hyde—inhabiting one body. There is but one agent. Some of his higher-order desires are wholesome and some of...

positions.title: MMSE-132 Position
positions.thesis: One tends to enjoy the activities that one values. I value playing the piano, and I also enjoy it; and these facts are obviously closely linked. But the relation I bear to that activity by virtue of enjoying it is distinct from the relation that I bear to it by virtue of valuing it. By virtue of the fact that I value playing the piano, I bear a cognitive relation to that activity; in other words, my valuing it consists in my having made a judgment about it (viz., I see it as having value and therefore---for reasons that we’ll soon identify---as being agential). By virtue of the fact that I enjoy playing the piano, I bear a purely affective relation to it (viz., it makes me feel good). 2.3 What are values? A value is a judgment on the part of some person (or sentient creature) to the effect that a certain course of action, or a certain outlook, will increase the scope of its agency. A moment ago I was reading a book (The Scientific Image, by Bas Van Fraassen). I then had a sudden urge to read a different book (The Will to Power, by Nietzsche). I was driven by a whim. Van Fraassen’s book was illuminating; I was not wasting my time by reading it. And, although, for personal and professional reasons, I do at some point have to read Nietzsche’s book (which, judging from the fragments I’ve read, is extremely good), I would, in giving in to my whim to ditch Van Fraassen, be undermining a resolution of mine, and doing so for no good reason. The result of my ditching Van Fraassen under these circumstances would have been a degradation of psychological integrity. I would have been allowing my decision-making process to give undue weight to sudden fancies. I would have been sending a message to myself that it’s ok not to carry out an intention that I had formed in a rational and informed manner and that I knew that, if successfully carried out, would make me a better and more powerful person. This illustrates, and also supports, though only to a small degree, our contention that value is a judgment to the effect that a certain course of action or way of living increases the scope and power of one’s agency. I value carrying out rationally formed intentions. But why? Because I...

positions.title: MMSE-133 Position
positions.thesis: This objection is not hard to neutralize. Let VS be a value-system according to which one's primary ethical obligation is to augment the scope of other people's agency. One has no moral obligation to promote what has no moral value. So, one has no moral obligation to promote welfare (agency) unless agency has moral value. A fortiori one's primary moral obligation cannot be to promote other people's welfare unless agency is the most valuable thing. But agency is, of its very nature, something that has only a relative value: relative to me, my agency may well be the most valuable thing. (In fact, I have no doubt that it is.) But relative to you, my agency has no value at all--except in an irrelevant, purely instrumental sense. (All references to "value" in this context are to intrinsic, not instrumental, value.) So VS is saying that, ethically, what is worthless to a given individual is the very thing that has the most worth. And I do not see how such a value system could be a coherent one. This suggests that one of the following propositions holds: (i) What is of moral value may not be of the slightest value to any given individual, in which case anyone who acts morally is ipso facto acting irrationally. (In fact, even in cases where what is moral happens to coincide with what is valuable to a given agent, it is not as somebody who is acting morally that he is acting rationally. His behavior, if rational, merely coincides with that of somebody who is genuinely moral. It does not itself embody any morality, being nothing more than a mere simulation of it.)

positions.title: MMSE-134 Position
positions.thesis: So a certain incoherence is internal to public value systems. But such systems must be incoherent in the just-described ways if they are to do their job. Public value systems exist to ensure that, on balance, people's rights aren't horribly violated. The only value systems that can accomplish this are ones that, from a logical viewpoint, are incoherent. (There is nothing incoherent in the supposition that a value system must be incoherent if it is to have the causal properties that a public value system ought to have. This is because how much merit a value-system has is to be judged by the effects on people’s lives through implementing it, not by its coherence. Value systems are not scientific doctrines. Thus, a welfare-maximizing viewpoint may be incoherent.) Setting aside people who are simply deluded (e.g., people who believe that joining cult X,a precondition for which is wire-transferring their life-savings to the Master’s Swiss Bank account, is the royal road to self-actualization), when people say that performing self-diminishing acts (e.g., working at a soup-kitchen) has "value," they may be speaking the truth; for what they are saying is that performing such acts, though not intrinsically valuable, is valuable in that it consolidates value-systems that are instrumental in preserving and augmenting agency in general. 3.0 Minds . Selves In this section I will make some points about the difference between minds and selves. What I say in this section will be obscure, and I will provide little or no argumentation for it. The contents of this section will be both clarified and justified in the sections following it. All selves are minds, but not all minds are selves. A self is a mind that is an agent. An agent is a mind that acts, and doesn’t just react. In order for a mind to be capable of acting, instead of just reacting, that mind must do more than simply facilitate the gratification of primal urges; it must do more than provide the organism with information concerning the external world that makes it clear how, without being killed or injured, that organism can eat, sleep, etc. A mind whose only objectives are to gratify such urges, and in which the sole purpose of knowledge is to enable such urges to be gratified, merely reacts, in light of the knowledge that it has, to the directives of instinct. Such a mind does not act....

positions.title: MMSE-135 Position
positions.thesis: and: One fact about LM’s occupants must be made explicit. There is nothing regulating their exchanges with one another. Should one LM-occupant defraud another, there would be no penalties. Given that there are no laws in LM, the victim would have no legal recourse and he’d have to take it upon himself to see that justice be done. An LM-occupant who’d been misinformed by another would be in the same situation mutatis mutandis. It may be that, because LM’s occupants are so honest with one another, they’re better off for the fact that their lives aren’t weighed down by the presence of the regulatory entities that would be needed for there to be any formal penalties for fraud. But what is relevant here is that, precisely because their lives are not thus “weighed down," the occupants of LM do not jointly constitute a nation, at least not in the sense in which France constitutes a nation. There is nothing under whose jurisdiction the occupants of LM fall.[463]

positions.title: MMSE-136 Position
positions.thesis: ‘mental entity that we are directly conscious of.’ 2.1 How is repression possible? (continued) Given (3), it immediately follows that there is a sense in which all mental entities that aren’t constituents of consciousness are unconscious. Your knowledge that 1 + 1 = 2 is not a constituent of consciousness. So far as it is conscious, it is only in the sense that you can readily become conscious of it. And most of the time you are not conscious of it. But it exists, and is operative, even when you are not conscious of it. If you didn’t know that 1 + 1 = 2, then the way in which you dealt with the world---the way in which you transacted with others, as well as the way you dealt with problems not having an interpersonal dimension----would differ radically from the way in which, even when you’re not consciously thinking that 1 + 1 = 2 or a fortiori thinking about your knowledge that 1 + 1 = 2, you in fact deal with the world. The way in which you reason is not only consistent with, but expressive of, a knowledge that 1 + 1 = 2, the same being true of the way in which you interact with people and also of the way in which you manage matters that don’t involve others. But it is only during, at most, a tiny few of the occasions when one’s thoughts and overt acts are guided by a conscious awareness of the fact that 1 + 1 = 2. It is only rarely that one consciously thinks about the fact that 1 + 1 = 2 or, therefore, that one thinks about one’s belief that 1 + 1 = 2. Bearing this in mind, suppose, if only for argument’s sake, that your knowledge that 1 + 1 = 2 is identical with some physiological structure. It follows from what we’ve said that, even when you are not conscious of the fact 1 + 1 = 2, or therefore of your knowledge of that fact, this belief of yours is psychologically operative. Of course, given that it’s a physiological structure, it’s inevitably operative in the same, thought-free way as an involuntary muscle-spasm. But, even when not the object of consciousness, that physiological structure is also psychologically active; it’s active, in other words, as a belief, and not just as a heap of...

positions.title: MMSE-137 Position
positions.thesis: and: (iii) you know that, in virtue of these facts, you have two distinct conceptions of some one individual. But this changes. For, at time t, you find out that Ted Jones is the Springfield Strangler. You see him being arrested. You hear the subsequent news reports. You consider various facts directly known to you about Ted Jones: his sudden nightly disappearances, which typically involved Ted’s dragging large trunks to his pick-up truck; the stench emanating from his house, which he implausibly explained by saying that he had a clogged sink; etc. You thus realize that Ted Jones is indeed the Springfield Killer. Just as one can, without knowing it, be acquainted with a given individual while also having descriptive knowledge of it, so one can be acquainted with a given one of one’s own mental contents while also having descriptive knowledge of it. A corollary is that, given some mental entity E that belongs to one, one can discover that one actually has some attitude towards E that one did not previously know oneself to have. You can discover that the gnawing feeling of unpleasantness you experience whenever somebody mentions Smith is your detestation of Smith. Emotions are distinct from their manifestations in consciousness. Your love of your mother (supposing for argument’s sake that she’s alive and that you do love her) is an enduring psychological structure; it is not an ephemeral event. But it gives rise to ephemeral conscious events (to pangs of remorse when you inadvertently hurt her feelings, to surges of joy when, after not hearing from her for three months, she calls you, etc.). But your love for her is not itself a conscious event. It is "conscious" only in the Pickwickian sense that you are conscious of it. Beliefs, like emotions, are enduring structures and are to be distinguished from the constituents of consciousness to which they may give rise. All propositional attitudes are enduring structures and are thus to be distinguished from any constituents of consciousness for whose existence they’re responsible. And a given emotion, e.g., your hatred of Smith, can give rise to conscious events of very different kinds. It can give rise to gnawing feelings of unpleasantness (as when you hear that he’s coming over), to intense feelings of satisfaction (as when you hear that he flunked his economics exam), etc. And given two qualitatively distinct conscious events, both of them...

positions.title: MMSE-138 Position
positions.thesis: Thus, the exchange of information between minds involves non-mental intermediaries and, for that reason, is in some important sense “indirect.” But information-transmissions that occur within a mind is either direct or, if it isn’t direct, consists solely of direct information-transmissions (i.e. that consist of sequences of information-transmissions, each one of which is direct, even though, obviously, those direct transmissions jointly constitute an indirect transmission). I know that Jerry is a vicious and prolific murderer. All of a sudden, I see Jerry’s grinning face pressed against my window. The result: I’m afraid. (I wouldn’t have reacted with fear if my pre-existing belief had been to the effect that Jerry was a harmless prankster who, in his attempts to be funny, pressed his grinning face against people’s windows.) Clearly, the information encoded in my knowledge about Jerry is “pooled” with the contents of my sense-perception, that being why I am struck by fear. That pooling occurs without anyone’s engaging in any overt behaviors and, therefore, without anyone’s employing any symbolic intermediaries. The one mental entity’s contents directly merge (if that’s the right word) with the others. The fact that these two bodies of information can merge thus directly is not a consequence of their both belonging to the same mind: it is constitutive of their belonging to the same mind. Two mental entities whose contents are thus related ipso facto both “belong” to some one mind. Of course, there are distinct mental entities that belong to some one mind that are not as directly related to each other as are the two just-mentioned mental entities. For example, none of the mental entities constitutive of my mind in 1987 is related in that way to any given one of my current mental contents. And there are mental entities in my mind right at this very moment that are not thus interrelated. But, given one of those 1987-mental entities, how are its contents pooled with those of a given one of my 2010 mental contents? Through overt behaviors on my (or somebody else’s) part? No. Through symbolic intermediaries? No. The pooling is accomplished via a series of mental states S1…Sn such that S1’s contents directly merge with those of the aforementioned 1987 mental entity, such that Sn’s contents directly merge with those of the aforementioned 2010 mental entity, and such that, for each i, the contents of Si directly merge with those of Si+1....

positions.title: MMSE-139 Position
positions.thesis: Rationalizers see the world as they want it to be, not as it is. Thinkers see the world as it is not as they want to be. So people have an incentive both to rationalize and to not think. Also, rationalizing is the path of least resistance. Working for what you want is hard; pretending that you have it is easy. This obviously gives people another incentive both to rationalize and to not think. Also, thinking does, whereas rationalization does not, involve recognizing that one is fallible and, therefore, that the world was not designed to validate one's opinions. Thinking involves subordinating one's mental activities to objective norms. This involves evaluating one's own thoughts and, oftentimes, admitting that they're wrong. Rationalization involves not subordinating one's thoughts to objective norms and not admitting that one is wrong. So the rationalizer's ego is spared the hard knocks that the thinker's ego has to endure. Rationalization is a manifestation of our instinct to flee from danger. Through physical flight, we flee from an external threat; through rationalization, we flee from an internal threat.[482] Thus, a person's tendency to rationalize has deep instinctual roots. For this reason, rationalizing doesn't involve exercising one's agency. In fact, it involves letting sub-agential, instinctual forces triumph over one's agency. So far as one's agency is involved in the construction of a rationalization, it is nothing more than the puppet of sub-agential instincts, its sole function being to make instinct-driven, lapses in self-discipline and rationality appear to oneself to be expressions of rationality and self-control. Of course, everyone rationalizes, even the greatest of thinkers. But to the extent that one’s mental activity mediates rationalization, that activity is sub-agential; and to the extent that one’s mental activity mediates genuine thought, it is agential. These points are conjectural, it must be emphasized. Nonetheless, I wish to end this section with a point that is even more conjectural than those just made. We all know people who are extraordinarily “street smart”, even though their discursive abilities are those of somebody whose level of intelligence is extremely low. Speaking personally, I used to believe that a street smart person was an intelligentwho didn't have the good fortune to receive a decent education; I used to believe that, if a given street smart person had been properly educated, he would be as discursively intelligent as, in actuality, he was street smart. Having had a...

positions.title: MMSE-140 Position
positions.thesis: (vi) Vampires can turn into bats. (vii) One becomes a vampire by selling one’s soul. (viii) A vampire must drink the blood of the living; otherwise he dies. (ix) Vampires are parasites and thus have only a borrowed, second-class existence. (This follows from (viii).) . Taken jointly, (i)-(ix) suggest that vampires represents sociopaths. Interpretation of (i) and (ii): Vampires have no substance. That’s why they cease to exist in the light of day, and it’s why they don’t appear in mirrors. Like vampires, sociopaths have no substance. That’s why, when scrutinized---when looked at in the light of day, as it were, and not allowed to take refuge in darkness---the sociopath’s personality vanishes, his morality and indeed his entire personality proving to be nothing more than fictions. Interpretation of (iii): The vampire’s face represents the sociopath’s self-concept, and the vampire’s inability to see his own face represents the fact that the sociopath has no endogenous self-concept. Lacking in any real self-concept, sociopaths are empty; there is no life in them. Interpretation of (iv): The fact that only others can see the vampire’s face corresponds to the fact that the sociopath’s self-concept is dependent on the acknowledgement of others. (Sociopaths tend to spent a lot of time in front looking at themselves in the mirror.) Interpretation of (v): The sociopath is empty; he tries to fill the void with external tokens of the success. Also, since can’t distinguish the image from the reality, and wealth creates the image of success, the sociopath equates wealth with success. Interpretation of (vi): Bats are blind. So are sociopaths. Sociopaths believe what they want to believe, not what is real; they are blind to the truth. Interpretation of (vii): One becomes a sociopath by selling out. Interpretation of (viii) and (ix): The sociopath’s existence is a waking death, from which he has no respite except when he’s drinking up the adulation of others, a corollary being that, without the adulation, the sociopath suffers the same fate as the blood-deprived vampire.

positions.title: MMSE-141 Position
positions.thesis: or (iii) such praise brings with it all manner of monetary and practical benefits thanks to which one doesn’t have to waste time groveling before slimy editors and working at a menial job and thanks to which one finally has access to some of the finer things in life. But this only confirms our point that, if a person genuinely is convinced that what he’s written is good, he doesn’t want adulation per se: what he wants are the benefits that adulation brings with it. Some are likely to reject (i). “If an author knows that what he’s written is good,” it will be said, “he ipso facto cannot possibly be at all insecure about its merits.” This criticism falsely assumes that people cannot have mutually opposed beliefs. The statement “Smith believes that 1+1=2 and Smith does not believe that 1+1=2” is incoherent. But the statement “Smith believes that 1+1=2 and Smith believes that 1+1.2” is perfectly coherent. In fact, the history of ideas is replete with cases of people having incompatible beliefs. In particular, many a great artist (e.g. Brahms) was rightly convinced, in a genuine and non-defense way, of the excellence of his work while being tormented by terrible insecurities about it. People are not cognitively unified. And an author who knows that his work is great can, at some level, believe that his work is bad and, therefore, will find praise for his to work to be a source of comfort.

positions.title: MMSE-142 Position
positions.thesis: This reasonable-seeming analysis embodies several confusions. (I should point out that, in his excellent book The Faces of Intention, Michael Bratman makes points similar to, but not coincident with, those that I am about to make.[488]) A desire that is directly converted into overt behavior is a reaction, not an action proper. For a desire to lead to action, it must be inhibited and scrutinized, so as to ensure that it embodies the right values. By virtue of surviving such scrutiny, a desire becomes an intention and, to the extent that circumstances allow, automatically discharges itself in action. Even those desires that are expressed in action despite not surviving such scrutiny are not cases of desires being directly converted into action. They are cases of desires being scrutinized, then found to fall short, but being allowed to discharge themselves anyway. The reason the tobacco-addict feels ashamed is that, despite what his conscience tells him as he’s considering his wish to smoke, he nonetheless lifts the initial inhibition that he has on smoking. And even though, in respect of his overt behavior, he may pounce on the cigarette the way a tiger pounces on red meat, the addict’s behavior is that of somebody in whom the impulse to smoke is the result of a decision: his impulse is initially quarantined, then scrutinized, and only then, despite its falling short of the relevant valuative benchmarks, allowed to be discharged in action. The tiger’s desire, on the other hand, is directly translated into action. Where selves are concerned, few so-called impulsive behaviors, if any, involve a desire’s being directly translated into action; they typically involve cases where there is an initial inhibition which, despite his better judgment, the person in question chooses to lift. That said, the decision to lift the inhibition sometimes is a wise one. The person in question wishes to appear like a violent, unpredictable animal, since he knows that, given the circumstances, this is to his strategic advantage; and he knows that, if he punches the person who just insulted him, that is precisely how he will appear. Many apparently impetuous actions are carefully considered. We must remember that “carefully considered” . “slowly considered.” A carefully considered decision can be made virtually instantaneously. This is partly because we think fast. But it’s primarily because, given previous decisions that we spent a great deal of time making and that we’ve...

positions.title: MMSE-143 Position
positions.thesis: Nietzsche infamously argued for a doctrine known as ethical egoism (EE). According to this doctrine, it is one’s sole ethical obligation to promote one’s own welfare. One important desideratum that an ethical theory must satisfy is to say how people should treat others. Obviously EE fails to satisfy this desideratum. Since no viable ethical theory can fail to satisfy this desideratum, EE fails as an ethical theory. But there are desiderata that an ethical theory ought to satisfy and that, unlike every other ethical theory, EE does satisfy. First of all, an ethical theory shouldn’t require people to diminish themselves. The most pure way of being evil towards someone is to diminish them. An ethical theory that diminishes those who follow it is therefore evil. And an ethical theory that is itself evil is ipso facto not a coherent one. Aside from EE, all ethical theories do require people to diminish themselves: they require people to do what others want and need them to do, not what they want and need to do. EE doesn’t require people to diminish themselves. For EE’s sole injunction is: Don’t diminish yourself! EE isn’t evil towards those who follow it. Thus, there is a respect in which EE is coherent and in which all other ethical theories are incoherent. (That said, there are respects in which EE is incoherent and in which most, if not all, other ethical theories are coherent.) There is another important desideratum that EE satisfies and that all other ethical theories fail to satisfy. People are self-centered. People who don’t allow themselves to do what they want to do are frustrated, unhealthy people. (When I talk about “what people want to do," I mean what people want to do in so far as what they want to do isn’t the result of cowardice or complacency or otherwise self-diminishing factors. In other words, I am talking about the wishes that people’s selves have, and I am therefore not talking about the craven impulses that, although in people, are not of them---i.e., that, although in people’s minds, are not constitutive of their selves.) One can obey EE faultlessly without jeopardizing one’s psychological health. The same cannot be said of any other ethical theory. All other ethical theories require one to put other people’s interests ahead of one’s own. And, setting aside people who are already severely damaged, nobody can do this unremittingly....

positions.title: MMSE-144 Position
positions.thesis: In urging acceptance of (i)-(iv), Einhorn was really urging acceptance of (a)­(d), and he was thus imploring people to understand the world in the same emotion-driven, projective manner as our cave-dwelling ancestors. Thus, Einhorn’s supposed progressiveness was the purest form of regressivism. Because Einhorn was representative in this respect of an entire generation of so-called liberals, people who don’t want to return to a tribal mode of existence are given an incentive to not be liberal, even if they haven’t a conservative bone in their body. I feel that a few remarks about my personal experiences may not be out of order, so long as it’s kept in mind that those remarks may well be nothing more than prejudice-driven interpretations of facts about my life that, even if interpreted dispassionately, would reveal little about the world. I personally have known many leading members of the 1960’s counterculture; a very close relative of mine is one of the two or three best known astrologers in the nation. These particular individuals are among the most ill-willed and intellectually rigid people that I know. And, having attended many functions over which they presided, I have found the same to be true of their followers. For this reason, I suspect that acceptance of 1960’s-style liberalism often has roots in one, or both, of the following factors: (1) A wish to disguise, both from oneself and from others, a virulent, all-consuming ill-will, and: (2) A combination of a rabid desire for power, coupled with a narcissistic unwillingness to put in the work required to achieve success within institutions where they don’t make the rules and where they’ll be subjected to meaningful scrutiny. 6.4.1 Wittgenstein Einhorn was a big Wittgenstein fan. This was no accident. Wittgenstein was a psychopath. A below average student in both high-school and college, he received a PhD from Cambridge without taking any of the standard entrance-exams, without taking a single class, and without writing so much as a single paper, apart from the TLP. He submitted the TLP as his dissertation and, on that basis, was “awarded” a doctorate in Philosophy. Wittgenstein supposedly labored over the TLP. If this is true, his efforts were to no avail. The end result was an absolute failure. It’s maximally obscure, except when what it is saying isn’t trivial or obviously false. No effort is made to clarify the obscure statements or to substantiate them....

positions.title: MMSE-145 Position
positions.thesis: Wittgenstein’s professional success was based entirely on the air of mystique that he cultivated. His post-Tractarian work consisted entirely in his blocking off fruitful avenues of inquiry. He insisted, in fact, that any attempt to solve any philosophical problem was ipso facto misguided. In the work in which he made that claim, he said, without ever saying what he meant, that “everything is as it appears” and “there are no mysteries.” He also says, without clarifying what he means, that “the purpose of philosophy is to show the fly the way out of the bottle.” One can see how one could see great depth in such statements. But one could also see how one could project depths into those statements that they don’t have. In fact, there is little, if anything, in Wittgenstein’s work that, when interpreted literally as opposed to projectively, has any meaning at all. Wittgenstein’s native language was German, not English. He never became fluent and always spoke with a heavy accent. This, I suspect, was much of the reason he wasn’t seen for what he was. It immediately disposed people not to take what he was saying at face value, to assume that his obscure utterances bore hidden meanings, and, consequently, to project meanings into words that weren’t there. Wittgenstein spoke little English when he first met Russell. But, even though Russell spoke fluent German, Wittgenstein insisted on speaking English. This is because Wittgenstein knew that, had he spoken in German, Russell would have seen that his failure to say anything meaningful was a result, not of his being unable to articulate his thoughts, but of his not having any. Also, because at that time Wittgenstein's English was so poor and because, as a result, he wasn't saying anything meaningful, Wittgenstein's words were, I more than suspect, a blank a canvass onto which Russell liberally projected his own insights, this being why, despite initially believing Wittgenstein to be an imbecile and a phony, Russell came to see great depths in Wittgenstein's broken sentences. During his lectures, Wittgenstein would pause for unusually long periods of time. During these pauses, his face and bodily movements projected deep pensiveness along with an almost maniacally intense level of concentration. To a large extent, I suspect, these silences were Wittgenstein’s way of avoiding having to say anything and, therewith, of allowing his audience-members to project into his fragmentary speech the very...

positions.title: MMSE-146 Position
positions.thesis: In their (2001) book The Compositionality Papers, Jerry Fodor and Ernie Lepore put forth several blistering criticisms of CRS. In that work, any gaps in the criticisms of CRS just put forth are very much filled in. Conditional: A conditional statement is one that has the form if P, then Q. Conditional statements play a vital role in logic, since logic is the discipline that attempts to determine what follows from what, and any statement to the effect that one statement follows from another is expressed by a conditional. Confirm: For P to confirm Q is for P to provide support for Q. (P: ‘Smith’s fingerprints are found in the murder-weapon used to kill Jones.’ Q: “Smith killed Jones.” P supports Q.) Confirmation is defeasible, meaning that, even if P confirms Q, some other statement might provide more support for not-Q than P provides for Q. (R: “There is a video-tape of Brown killing Smith, then pressing the hand of Smith, who is at the crime-scene but unconscious, around the murder-weapon, and then leaving it at the scene.”) Entailment is not defeasible. A consequence is that P cannot, if true, entail Q without Q’s being true. See “confirmation.” Confirmation: P confirms Q if, supposing that P is true, it’s possible that Q is false but it’s more likely than not that Q is true. “Smith is a Harvard professor” confirms “Smith is not a complete moron,” but it doesn’t entail it. See “confirm” and “entailment.” Conjunct: In P and Q, each of P and Q is a “conjunct.” So snow is white is one of the conjuncts of snow is white and grass is green. See “conjunction.” Conjunction: A conjunction is any sentence composed of two sentences that are joined by an “and” or a “but” or an “although”—or any other connective whose purpose is to indicate that both of the sentences thereby joined are true. Connective: A “connective” expression is one that, given one or more sentences, enables a new sentence to be formed. So, “if ” is a connective since, given the sentences “grass is green” and “snow is white,” it yields the sentence “if grass is green, then snow is white.” All conjunctions (“and,” “or,” “but”) are connectives. So is “not.” This is because “snow is not white” says the same thing as “it is not the case that snow is white,” which is what results when...

positions.title: LMCC-001 Position
positions.thesis: The misunderstandings that dominate contemporary philosophy of language have crystallized in an argument known as the “Slingshot”. The Slingshot “proves” things that are obviously not true (e.g. that your car is identical with my computer). So we must look for errors in it. These are not hard to find. Once these errors are exposed, it becomes necessary to rethink and reconstruct many semantic orthodoxies. The purpose of this work is to effect that reconstruction.
