What kinds of properties does a physical state of affairs or object have apart from its compositional properties? Well, such things also have causal properties. And things with very different compositional properties can have, at least up to a point, the same causal properties. It wouldn’t be hard to create something that, to a high degree of approximation, had the same causal properties as the carburetor in your car, but was made out of a completely different kind of material. This leads us to functionalism: what makes x be (e.g.) a desire for water is not that it is made of this or that kind of tissue, but that it has the right causal properties: it is caused by certain things and, in its turn, causes certain other things. x is caused by dehydration and causes you to ingest the contents of glasses of ice-waters: and x is a sensation of thirst wholly in virtue of having [185] those cause and effects. The same is true mutatis mutandis for every other mental thing. What makes brainstate y be (or realize)...

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-032 Position
positions.thesis: * Conceptso (note the subscript) are atoms. They cannot be broken down into other conceptso. Consequently, there are (with some trivial exceptions) [197] no analytic truths. As we discussed, it certainly seems that, if one grasps the concepto knowledge, one also grasps the concepto true belief. This is exactly what Fodor denies. In his view, one could grasp the concepto knowledge without grasping any other conceptso. Fodor admits that there are some exceptions to this. A grasp of the concepto red cow is not an atom, as it plainly consists of a grasp of the concepto red and also the concepto cow. The same is true of the concepto prime number. If the concepto one is grasping is patently synthesized out of [198] other conceptso, then a grasp of it is not an atom. But with these exceptions, one’s grasp of a concepto is always an atom. Fodor’s denial of (i) is connected with his denial of (ii). Fodor thinks that conceptso (with the trivial exceptions just given) have no internal structure. The concepto knowledge has no constitutive connections to the conceptso belief or truth or justification. Of course, if Fodor is right about that, then we can see why a grasp of knowledge wouldn’t presuppose a grasp of these other conceptso. Fodor obviously admits that conceptso typically appear to have a great deal of analytic structure. Obviously knowledge is always true appears to be analytic; and so do 2 comes before 3 and causes precede their effects. He goes to great and ingenious lengths to explain away this appearance of [199] analyticity. We will consider these efforts soon enough. Fodor grants that there are some analytic truths. The concepto red cow is “patently phrasal”, as Fodor puts it. It is obviously built out of other conceptso (red and cow). Any such concepto will clearly be associated with various analytic truths. (x is a red cow implies, and is implied by, the conjunction x is red and x is a cow.) Fodor grants the existence of analyticities of that kind. And Fodor grants that x is a cow entails x is a cow. Notice that those analyticities are trivial. They don’t expose any hidden depths on the part of the relevant conceptso. In Fodor’s view, all analytic truths are in the same category as: (*) if x is a red cow, then x is a cow. We can be a...

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-033 Position
positions.thesis: * something x is uniquely being a last celestial body to disappear fromthe morning sky, and something y is uniquely being a last celestial body to disappear from the morning sky, and x=y. Here is the apparent problem for our analysis. The proposition: (AN) Necessarily, Hesperus is uniquely a last celestial body to disappear from the morning sky, is simply false, and nobody thinks otherwise. But what about (B)? At first glance, (B) looks like the kind of proposition that might be necessary. If that is the case, then our analysis is false. The truth is that our analysis does not put us on the wrong side of any modal facts. Let us consider the operator necessarily. This operator can combine in different ways with (B): more exactly, it can be given varying degrees of scope. (B1) Necessarily: something x is uniquely a last celestial body to disappear from the morning sky, and x is identical with a unique a last celestial body to disappear from the morning sky. (B2) Something x is uniquely a last celestial body to disappear from the morning sky, and necessarily: x is identical with a unique a last celestial body to disappear from the morning sky. (B1) and (B2) are simply false; and nobody would ever think otherwise. So in the relevant respects, they just like (AN). So our analysis does not turn true modal claims into false ones, or vice versa. Nonetheless, it might seem that our analysis has a peculiar consequence. We have said that you think of Hesperus through either (****) or through some other proposition of the same form. So it seems that, on our analysis, you must think of Hesperus a certain way: you must think of it as a last celestial body to disappear from the morning sky or a first celestial body to appear in the evening sky: in any case, you must think of it through some uniquely individuating description. But this seems wrong: for there doesn’t seem to be any that anyone has to think of Hesperus. For reasons we’ve seen, it seems that cognitively “locking onto” a thing O involves knowing some proposition of the form something x uniquyely has phi, where O has phi. But after that lock-on has been established, I don’t have to think about O by way of that proposition. I can drop the proposition. Suppose that I lock onto...

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-034 Position
positions.thesis: * “Supervenience Revisited” in Essays in Quasi-Realism Oxford 1993 Cheshire Calhoun and Robert C. Solomon editors What is an Emotion? Oxford University Press 1984 Hume, David A Treatise Concerning Human Nature Edited, with an Analytical Index, by L.A. Selby-Bigge; Second Edition, with text revised and variant readings by P.H. Nidditch. Oxford at the Clarendon Press 1978 Merleau-Ponty, Maurice The Phenomenology of Perception Routledge 1962 Sartre, Jean-Paul The Emotions: A Sketch of a Theory 1939 The Psychology of the Imagination translated by Bernard Frechtman Washington Square Press 1948 Stevenson, C.L. [1] “The Emotive Meaning of Ethical Terms” in Logical Positivism ed. A.J. Ayer The Free Press 1959 NO SUCH THINGS AS MENTALESE Abstract According to Jerry Fodor, for someone to grasp a proposition – to have a thought – is just for some sentence of an internal language to be tokened. This internal language is thus the ‘language of thought’. In this essay I will provide some reasons to think that this idea is untenable. For someone to know a language L, that person must know the rules giving meaning to the expressions of L. (Admittedly, this idea may be questioned; but reasons are provided for it.) So for someone to know the language of thought, he must know the rules which assign meaning to the expressions belonging to that language. Now we know from Tarski that no language can express the rules that give its own expressions meaning. So if one is to grasp the semantical rules for some language L, one must either grasp them (i) in some ‘Meta’-language (that comprises L as a proper part of itself) or (ii) one must grasp them in some altogether non-linguistic form. Someone who holds that all thinking consists in the tokening of symbols cannot take path (ii). So he must take path (i). This means that if one is to understand a language L, one must understand the relevant meta-language and, therefore, must grasp the rules that give meaning to the expressions of this meta-language. But by Tarski’s theorem, these rules cannot be given in the meta-language itself. So if one is to grasp these rules – and, therefore, if one is to understand the meta-language – these rules must be given to one either (i’) in some meta-meta-language or (ii’) in some nonlinguistic form. If we adopt path (ii’) we are abandoning the idea that thinking consists in the tokening...

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-035 Position
positions.thesis: * If pigs are the only flying creatures, and only flying creatures havehorns, then only pigs have horns. You now know what proposition (i) encodes. But you don’t really understand the sentence, at least not in the sense in which a Finn does. For you have no idea what contribution the various components of (i) make to the proposition expressed by that sentence. So at least one of the dimensions of (i)’s meaning that is known to any Finn is unknown to you. We can characterize this difference by saying that for you (i) is a primitive expression, whereas for a Finn (i) is a highly complex expression. So even though the proposition expressed by (i) is complex, nevertheless the sentence itself is, from your point view (though not a Finn’s), semantically primitive: for, from your point of view (though not a Finn’s) its meaning is not to be arrived by performing a computation on the meanings of simpler expressions. Now suppose that Fodor is right: suppose that you (or the relevant subpersonal agency within you) doesn’t understand Mentalese sentences by consulting any set of rules; that you are simply ‘so constructed’ that you understand them. (Incidentally, the understanding of Mentalese sentences, if there are such things, occurs at an entirely sub-personal level. So, strictly speaking, it is incorrect to speak of you processing such sentences; for this term obviously refers to a person, and not to a network of sub-personal computational processes. But for brevity’s sake, I will speak of ‘you’ grasping, processing etc. Mentalese sentences. Simply realize that, in these contexts, I am using the term ‘you’ to refer to some sub-personal entity within you.) This means that, given any Mentalese sentence S, you don’t compute its meaning. In that case, no Mentalese sentence that you come across is semantically complex, no matter how complex the proposition that it encodes. Given this, consider the English sentence

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-036 Position
positions.thesis: * cows like grass. It is pretty obvious, then, that you arrive at the meaning of (i) by, in some way or other, ‘integrating’ the meanings of these other sentences. And, of course, your ‘integrating’ these meanings consists in your applying some set of rules that are defined over these meanings. In light of this, consider the Mentalese sentence: (i’) If snow is white, and all cows eat snow, and all things that eat white things like grass, then cows like grass. One would presume that you can understand (i’) by virtue of understanding the Mentalese sentences (ii’) snow is white (iii’) all cows eat snow; (iv’) all things that eat white things like grass; and (v’) cows like grass. But if Fodor is right, this is not the case. If Fodor is right, (i’) is not – at least nor from your point of view: from the point of view the person who has to figure out what (i’) means - semantically complex; its meaning is not to be computed in any way. For computation involves consideration of rules, and Fodor’s way of blocking the regress discussed above is to say that one understands Mentalese sentences without consulting any rules. A fortiori, as far as you can tell –- for, if Fodor’s objection is correct, you have no access to the recursions relating (i’) to (ii’)-(v’) -- (i’)’s meaning is not a function of the meanings of (ii’)-(v’). But this idea is multiply incoherent. For Fodor – for anyone who believes that there is a language of thought - grasping the proposition snow is white simply is grasping the Mentalese sentence (ii’) snow is white, and grasping the proposition If snow is white, and all cows eat snow, and all things that eat white things like grass, then cows like grass simply is grasping the Mentalese sentence (i’) If snow is white, and all cows eat snow, and all things that eat white things like grass, then cows like grass. For Fodor, you have no way of grasping the propositions other than by grasping these sentences of Mentalese. (This is what the whole ‘language-of-thought’ hypothesis amounts to.) Now there’s obviously no way to grasp the proposition (i) without grasping (ii)-(v): your grasp of (i) is surely ‘built up’ in some way from your grasp of (ii)-(v). For Fodor, grasping (i) simply is to grasp (i’). So, if Fodor is...

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-037 Position
positions.thesis: * A person is not wholly constituted by the mental contents which he has. A person is, at least in part, something which underlies – or, in any case, stands apart from – his mental contents. (i) has been criticized on a number of grounds. My objective in this paper is to show that one of these criticisms can be met, i.e. that one of these criticisms does not invalidate the bundle theory. It is by no means a weak criticism. (Indeed, given how much work we will have to do to neutralize it, it is clear that it is quite powerful.) The criticism in question is essentially this:

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-038 Position
positions.thesis: * Clearly some pairs of mental contents belong to the same mind while others do not. Your feeling of thirst and your perception of the ice-water belong to the same mind. By contrast, my feeling of thirst and your perception of the ice-water do not belong to the same mind. Supposing that the bundle theory is correct, what is it that makes two mental contents belong to the same mind? The proponent of (**) has no trouble dealing with this problem: two mental belongs to the same mind just in case they are had by the same self. But the bundle theorist cannot avail himself of this response. If he did, his position would be circular. According to the bundle theorist, a self just is certain class of mental contents. So for a mental content to belong to a certain self is just for that mental content to have a certain relation to other mental contents. Let R be this relationship, whatsoever it might turn out to be. R cannot be defined in terms of the notion of f ‘being had by the same self’ since, for the bundle theorist, it is this very relation that R is meant to explain. So the bundle theorist must say what R is without helping himself to the notion of ‘belonging to the same self.’ But there is no way – or, at any rate, no non-circular way -- to do this. With regard to every attempt to define R independently of the notion of being had by the same self, every such attempt has failed outright or has involved some kind of covert circularity. [248] My objective in this essay is to show that R can be defined independently of the notion of ‘being had by the same self’. In other words, I wish to show that we can say what it is for two mental contents to belong to the same mind without any circularity – without appealing to the notion of ‘being had by the same self.’ In a word, we can show that the bundle theory is not guilty of [249] circularity. [250] By way of anticipation, my analysis is very roughly as follows. It is pretty clear that two mental contents that belong to the same mind have causal powers vis a vis one another that mental contents belonging to different minds do not. My mental contents can...

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-039 Position
positions.thesis: * that snow reflects light. So your believing P and Q will no doubt cause, or tend to cause, you to believe R. Moreover, your believing P and Q will justify your believing R. So in this case – and this is by no means exceptional – mental causation coincides with justification. We must be careful about one thing. For an inference to be made is not just for one belief to cause another belief to occur: the contents of those beliefs must be implicated in a certain way. My believing P and Q could cause me to believe R without my inferring R from P and Q. My brain might just be wired in such a way that, if I believe P and Q, then I automatically believe R. (Or a belief in P and Q might generate a belief in R by way of purely associative or emotional, as opposed to rational, processes.) For me to infer R from [P and Q], I must see the bearing of [P and Q] on R. So for me to really infer R from [P and Q], a grasp of the content of [P and Q] must be implicated in a certain way in the generation of the belief R. So suppose that B1 is my belief in the conjunctive proposition [P and Q], and that B2 is my belief in R. For me to really infer R from [P and Q], it is not enough that B1 cause B2 to occur. It is necessary, also, that B1’s being a belief that [P and Q] be causative of B2. (In other words, B1’s being a belief that snow is white and white things reflect light must be causative of the belief that snow reflects light.) For if B1’s being that kind of belief isn’t operative in the generation of B2, then the representational content of B1 – viz. the conjunction [P and Q] – won’t be implicated in the kind of way that it must be if the generation of B2 is to qualify as an inference from B1, rather than a mere product of B1. To put this another way: if the transition from B1 to B2 is to constitute an inference from [P and Q] to R, then surely it is necessary, not merely that B1 cause B2, but that B2’s having a certain representational content cause B2: to...

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-040 Position
positions.thesis: * “at time t, a certain rabid animal r is in place p and at time t*, which issoon after t, x will start rolling towards place p*; and unless, during the interval between t* and when said animal arrives at p*, individuals x and y get into person z’s mini-van, x and y will be eaten alive.” even if the values t, p, and p* correspond to times and places indicated by somebody who uttered (i). Let’s assume that, in (i), the referent of “that obviously rabid animal” does in fact pick out a rabid and (for that reason, among others, dangerous) animal. Let T be a token of (i), and let T* be a token of (ii). It’s clear that, pretty much no matter what the circumstances are, the information borne by T is different—semantically and prudentially—from the information borne by T*. For this reason, and countless others of its ilk, a language that didn’t contain expressions like “that”, “here”, “I’, “now”---context-sensitive (indexical) expressions—would be quite useless (for everything other than, suggestively, making time- and place-insensitive statements about numbers and other denizens of Plato’s heaven). Bearing this in mind, let T# be a token of the same sentence-type as (i). But let’s assume that the occurrence, in T#, of “that obviously rabid animal” refers to an animal that, though rabid, is one tenth the size of a shrew, and also has no teeth and thus no way of biting (or otherwise transmitting rabies to) anyone, and is therefore no threat at all. In that case, clearly, T# is false, whereas T (so we may suppose) is correct. T and T* have the same syntax—on any delineation of that term. (Same grammar; same depth-grammar; same logical form.) But the proposition meant by T has very different entailment-relations from the proposition meant by T*. And, what is more on point, a creature that responded only to the forms of sentences would (other things being equal) respond to T in the same way that it would respond to T*. But (other things being equal) no person would respond to T in the same that he’d respond to T*. In general, beings that respond to sentence-token-form, and not (except in so far as sentence-form is (low-grade) sentence-semantics) to sentence-tokensemantics, are (i) not rational creatures and (ii) aren’t us (since, irrational though we often are, we tend to be irrational---we flee from tigers,...

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-041 Position
positions.thesis: * Exactly one person is being described, on some particular occasion t, as having phi. There are many cads. But there are many occasions where only one person is being described as a cad Your picking up the word “Argo” doesn’t involve your learning the truth of some claim like: exactly one person x is a cad, and “Argo” refers to x. Such a claim is false; there are many cads. Your picking up “Argo” involves your learning the truth of some claim: on some specific occasion, exactly one person x is being described as a cad, and “Argo” refers to x. We must take care to make one other distinction. Smith himself never uttered an existence claim of the form: there is some x such that x uniquely has phi and “Argo” refers to x. Smith himself never uttered an existence claim of the form: on occasion t, I (Smith) referred to somebody x as a cad and “Argo” refers to x. Smith said only “Argo is a cad”, “Peewee is a cad”, and so forth. But the information that you are given, by virtue of being in discussion with Smith, is not confined to what Smith himself says. When Smith says “Argo is a cad”, the information that is encoded in his words is quite threadbare. But you are given information above and beyond that information; you witness Smith’s speech act. You put that speech act within a larger context; you have an enormous amount of information about when that speech act occurs. You know that it occurred after certain other events (e.g. before Smith spilled his beer) and before certain other events (e.g. before Jones made the unseemly pass at the waitress). In any case, even though what Smith was saying might have been minimal, the information you have about Smith’s saying it – about the context in which that speech-act occurred -- is enormous; and that information does suffice to give you the right kind of uniquely individuating description. Smith’s words do not by themselves give you a uniquely individuating description that applies to Argo or Peewee or Ichabod. But there is something that does give you such a description: that something is your perception of the conversational context in which Smith uses the word “Argo”. § There is no vicious circularity in this account. Smith personally met Argo. He learned the meaning of “Argo” through...

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-042 Position
positions.thesis: * The sentence “triangles have three sides” is in a different category fromthe sentence “Smith is bald.” It would be incoherent to deny the former; it would be coherent to deny the latter. Consequently, what the former says must be the case, and what the latter says need not be the case. It is through sense-perception that we know Smith to be bald. We see that Smith is bald or we sense-perceive evidence of his baldness. But how do we know of the truth of what is said by “triangles have three sides”? We obviously don’t know it by inspecting triangular objects. It isn’t through empirical research that we know it. Rationalists say: it is through “pure reason” – through some non-sensory cognitive faculty – that we know that triangles have three sides. Empiricists cannot take this position. For empiricism is, by definition, the view that all knowledge is acquired through the senses. One way empiricists have dealt with necessary truths is by turning them into linguistic truths. Sentences like “triangles have three sides” are plausibly seen as being true “by definition”. If one were to say “triangles do not have [280] three sides”, one would be “sinning against language”, as Ayer put it ; one would be using words in deviant ways. So “triangles have three sides” is made true by facts about language – facts about how people use words. It is true wholly in virtue of “linguistic convention”.

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-043 Position
positions.thesis: * This theory is now (rightly) rejected. Later on we will discuss why it iswrong. What I’d like to do now is to show that, unless one believes in propositions – unless one believes that, in addition to tables and chairs and iguanas, there are propositions – one must advocate this false theory. Let us refer to people who believe in propositions as “propositionalists”, and to people who do not as “anti-propositionalists”. Once again consider the sentence: (T) “Triangles have three sides.” A propositionalist says: (T) is necessarily true because it has for its meaning a necessarily true proposition, namely: triangles have three sides. That proposition is made true by facts about mind-independent concepts. The concepts triangle, and three and side are so interrelated that (T) must come out true, given that it bears the proposition triangles have three sides. So language makes (T) be true only in the sense that it associates that sentence with a necessarily true proposition. That proposition is true independently of anything having to do with language. An anti-propositionalist cannot say that. He cannot say that what makes (T) be necessary is that it is associated with a necessarily true proposition. For the anti-propositionalist doesn’t believe in propositions at all. So the antipropositionalist has to figure out some other way of answering the question: What makes (T) be true? What options does the anti-propositionalist have? He cannot say that (T) is made true by facts about the interrelations of platonic entities – of properties or concepts (in the non-psychological sense). Why can he not say this? To take this line would be to grant the existence of propositions. Presumably, the proposition triangles have three sides, if it exists, just is some structure consisting of the concepts (or properties) triangle, side, and so forth; so it just is these objects, interrelated in a certain way. So if an anti-propositionalist says that interrelations among platonic entities are what make (T) be true, then he is conceding that propositions do exist, and he is no longer an antipropositionalist. Surely triangles have three sides, if it exists at all, is built up out of the concepts triangle, three, side and so on. The propositions triangles have three sides and Jim’s pool has the shape of a triangle – if they exist at all -have something in common, corresponding to the fact that triangle is a constituent of both of...

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-044 Position
positions.thesis: * It seems clear that, in some cases, brain-states realize mental contents. (Iwish the word “state” to be taken as broadly as possible – to apply to both enduring brain-structures and occurrent phenomena.) My belief that 1+1=2 is realized by certain brain-states. My belief that water is wet is realized by a different set of -brainstates. Let x be an arbitrary brain-state According to functionalism, for x to realize mental content C is for x to have a certain causal role. For x to have a certain causal role is, at least in part, for x to have such and such effects. So x realizes a belief that bread nourishes at least partly in virtue of the effects that x has – for example, it leads to my choosing to eat bread (in certain contexts). There is an obvious prima facie problem with functionalism. Mental content is causally efficacious. In any case, this is what we are inclined to think. My believing that bread nourishes is causally effective: it leads me to drive to the story and buy bread, to eat said bread, and so on. Given this, suppose that brain-state x has effects E1…En. x’s having C presumably has a causal hand in bringing about at least some of E1…En. As we just saw, supposing that x is a brain-state realizing your belief that bread nourishes, x’s being such a belief is what causes you to buy bread, eat it, and so forth. But if x’s having those effects is what x’s realizing that content consists in, then x’s realizing that content cannot possibly be what causes E1…En to come about. If x’s realizing a belief that bread nourishes consists in its causing me to drive to the store and buy bread, and to eat bread, and so on, then x’s realizing that belief cannot possibly be what causes me to drive to the story, to eat the just purchased bread, and so on. So if functionalism is right, my belief that bread nourishes ends up not having a causal hand in my going the store to buy bread; and, by an exactly similar argument, it ends up not having a causal hand in my wanting to eat bread, or bake bread, and so on. It ends up not having a causal hand in any of the contexts where, quite obviously, it does have a causal hand. The nature...

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-045 Position
positions.thesis: * Jackson and Pettit deal with these problems in a brilliant way. Wemust distinguish causal efficacy from causal relevance. In everyday discourse, we say things like the following. “The liquid in the glass froze (thus expanding); and that is what is caused the glass to shatter.” The truth is: what caused the glass to shatter is something much more specific; it is the fact that, in certain very specific places, certain very specific pressures were put on the walls of the container. Consider the statement: “The presence of a flame under the container caused the liquid to boil.” Well, what really caused the container to heat up is much more specific: certain specific displacements of energy led to some other specific displacements of energy; and so on. Not every displacement of energy constitutive of the flame caused the increase in temperature. It wasn’t really the flame which did the work: it was certain very specific micro-events constitutive of the flame. One last example: “The pianist hit a wrong note because somebody coughed too loud.” Well, what really caused the pianist to hit a wrong note is much more specific. Smith coughed too loud – it wasn’t just somebody Causes are much more specific than our statements typically indicate; and the things we describe as “causes” – somebody’s coughing too loud, a flame’s being underneath the container, the freezing of the liquid in the glass – are not really what is doing the causal work; there is always something much more specific going on; some specific molecule (or atom or quark…) bouncing off of some other molecule (or atom…). In fact, in the end, the only things that are doing causal work are specific, minute displacements of massenergy. The flame is much too expansive and gross an entity to be responsible for the fact that this, that, or any other specific part of the pan heats up; what heats up place x on the pan is always some specific mass-energy displacement But when we say things like “someone’s coughing caused the pianist to hit a wrong note”, “the presence of the flame caused the pan to heat up”, and “the freezing of the liquid caused the vessel to shatter”, what we are saying is surely not altogether wrong. There is plainly a sense in which it is true to say that somebody’s coughing is what caused the pianist to error, a...

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-046 Position
positions.thesis: A. It seems reasonable to suppose that, in some cases, brain-states realizemental contents. (I wish the word “state” to be taken as broadly as possible – to apply to both enduring brain-structures and occurrent phenomena.) My belief that 1+1=2 is realized by certain brain-states. My belief that water is wet is realized by a different set of brainstates. Let x be an arbitrary brain-state of mine; and suppose that x is identical with, or realizes, my belief that bread nourishes. According to functionalism, for x to realize that belief is for x to have a certain causal role. For x to have a certain causal role is for it to be caused by certain things (e.g. visual perceptions of people eating bread and subsequently appearing sated) and for x, in its turn, to cause certain other things (e.g. my choosing to eat bread when hungry). So, for functionalism, x is a belief that bread nourishes in virtue of (inter alia) the fact that x leads to my deciding to drive to the store to buy bread, to my eating said bread, to my teaching loved ones how to bake bread, and so forth. There is an obvious problem for functionalism. If x’s realizing a belief that bread nourishes consists in (inter alia) its leading to certain states of affairs – e.g. my choosing to eat bread, my deciding to drive to the breadstore when hungry, and so forth – then x’s having that content cannot be what causes those states of affairs to come about. If x’s having that content is its having a certain causal role, then x’s having that content cannot affect or govern its causal role; it cannot be what brings about my choosing to eat bread or my teaching my loved ones how to bake it. In a word, if content is causal role, then content cannot affect causal role: content ends up not having [290] a causal hand in contexts where, quite plainly, it does have such a hand.

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-047 Position
positions.thesis: * Another doctrine – content externalism – also seems to make contentinert. Smith and Twin-Smith are atom for atom duplicates. But Smith is thinking about Mary, while twin-Smith is thinking about twin-Mary. The reason is that Smith’s brain-state has one distal cause (Mary), while twin- Smith’s has another (twin-Mary). The difference in distal cause makes for a [291] difference in content. But this difference in content seems to be inert. What does all the work is local: the spatially and temporally local properties of Smith’s brain are what cause him to pick up the phone and dial those numbers; the same is true (mutatis mutandis) of Twin-Smith. The differences in distal causes are causally irrelevant: everything that does causal work is in the here and now – is lodged inside Smith’s and twin-Smith’s respective crania. It is irrelevant [292] that Smith’s state was distally caused by Mary, as opposed to twinMary or hologram-Mary or Robo-Mary. Smith’s state might as well have been caused by any one of them, so far as the causal properties of that state are concerned. So to the extent that mental content is to be understood in externalist terms – to the extent that Smith and twin-Smith have qualitatively different mental contents in virtue of their differing causal liasons -- mental content doesn’t do anything. Externalism thus appears to strip mental content of causal [293] power. III. Jackson and Pettit deal with these problems in a brilliant way. First, they distinguish causal efficacy from what they refer to as causal relevance. They then attempt to show that, given this distinction, we can reconcile externalism and functionalism with the presumed causal potency of mental content. First let us discuss the distinction between efficacy and potency. Consider the statement: “The presence of a flame under the container caused the liquid to boil.” Well, what really caused the container to heat up is much more specific: certain specific displacements of energy led to some other specific displacements of energy; and these displacements happened in a very specific region – a region coincident with, or vanishingly close to, the region occupied by the under-surface of the pan. Not every displacement of energy constitutive of the flame caused the increase in temperature. It wasn’t really the flame which did the work: it was certain very specific micro-events constitutive of the flame. Causes are much more specific than our statements typically indicate;...

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-048 Position
positions.thesis: * there is some liquid x such that x and x alone is H2O and such that, attime t, x and x alone is what scalded your finger; moreover, ‘water’ refers to x. And so on. To know what ‘water’ refers to, it is necessary only that you know some truth of the form: (n) there is some substance x such that x uniquely has property phi; ‘water’ refers to x. Since water uniquely has each of infinitely many different properties, (n) is true for each of infinitely many values of phi. Where any given one of these truths is concerned, knowing it is sufficient, but not necessary, for knowing what ‘water’ refers to and, therefore, for knowing the semantics of that expression. Since (n) is a sentence-schema, not a sentence proper, it does not itself express any one of these truths. But in what follows, take (n) to stand for an arbitrary one of them. Only the underlined part of n describes a semantic rule. The rest describes the information through which one grasps that rule. Anyone who knows the semantics of water knows this (though they might recognize what it is that they know in the statements just made). Let’s tell another story about Smith and Jones. Both are competent speakers of English. Smith knows that:

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-049 Position
positions.thesis: * there is some liquid x such that x is H2O and such that creatures with gillscan breathe when immersed in x and creatures with lungs cannot; moreover, ‘water’ refers to x. There is some liquid x such that individual’s sole intention in saying (w2) ‘water covers most of the Earth’s surface’ is to say: (w3) x covers most of the Earth’s surface. Neither wishes to affirm either that (w4) there is some liquid x such that x is what is in bathtubs and drinkingglasses, and such that ‘water’ refers to x; moreover, x covers most of the Earth’s surface, or that (w5) there is some liquid x such that x is H2O and such that creatures with gills can breathe when immersed in x and creatures with lungs cannot; moreover, x covers most of the Earth’s surface. Each individual’s intention in uttering w2 is to ascribe a certain property to a certain substance. They have the same substance in mind. They wish to attribute the same property to it. Neither wants to say anything about how he thinks about that substance. Neither wants to attribute any property to anything other than that substance. Thus, what Smith means when he says ‘water covers most of the Earth’s surface’ is exactly what Jones means when he utters the same sentence. And so long as each knows that the other agrees with him about what it is ‘water’ that refers to, any difference between them in respect of how they think about its referent will in no way jeopardize the one person’s ability to understand the other. To put it more formally: provided that, for some x, each believes that ‘water’ refers to x and each knows that the other believes this, the differences between these to individuals in respect of how they think about this substance won’t jeopardize their ability to communicate with each other. 1.2 Putnam’s semantic externalism (continued) These points make it clear why it is that Smith and Twin-Smith, though driven to make the very same noises by the very same internal conditions, are nonetheless affirming different propositions. Let’s suppose that when Smith was a child, his mother pointed to the liquid in the tub in which Smith regularly bathed, which Smith recognized as the liquid that he had to drink in order to live, and said: ‘That is water.’ The message that is communicated to Smith is...

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-050 Position
positions.thesis: * B’s being embedded in a totally different way in a completely differentsort of environment. (i) is not identical with A. (ii) is not identical with B. (I am sitting in a chair right now. But I’m not identical with the state of affairs of JMK’s sitting in a certain chair at 1:22 p.m. on April 9, 2009.) A’s being planet is identical with (i)’s being the case. (To be a planet is to be a thing of certain dimensions (etc.) that is embedded in a certain way in a certain sort of environment.) Since A’s being a planet is no more identical with A than I am identical with the fact that, at this instant, I’m sitting in a certain chair, and since the same thing mutatis mutandis holds of B, it would be deeply illogical to accept Burge’s judgment that A and B differ in their causal properties. In order for that judgment to be correct, A would have to be identical with the fact that it was embedded in a certain way in a certain sort of environment, and by parallel reasoning you would be identical with the fact that, right now, you are holding a certain book in your hands. But so far as it isn’t obviously false, this position borders on meaninglessness. Despite all these problems with content-externalism, and despite some [338] comparably gruesome ones that I haven’t even mentioned, contentexternalism still has some loyal followers, who are indefatigable in their attempts to make content-externalism work. To these tenacious individuals, I can only say, quoting the great Marion Barry: ‘Let it go!’ The Computational Theory of Mind These days, a doctrine known as the “Computational Theory of Mind” (CTM) is very popular, both among scholars (especially philosophers and computer scientists) and among the laity. CTM is a form of materialism, for reasons that will become clear. One would think that this doctrine would be informed by the same intellectual progressiveness that led to the creation and subsequent development of computer science. And, because of its liberal and confident use of words such as “compute” and “algorithm,” CTM has an impressive sheen of rigor. But we will find that, ironically, CTM involves a regression to archaic, animistic forms of thought---that, more specifically, it represents the recrudescence of the primitive, and spurious, thinking that the grumbling of thunder represents the wrath of the gods. Speaking more philosophically...

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-051 Position
positions.thesis: * "n is identical with n." If our thinking were syntax-driven, we couldn't recognize the differences in respect of logical form between (1) and (2) or between (3) and (4). We can recognize those differences. So our thinking is not syntax-driven. So CTM is wrong. Here is a similar line of thought. If, on July 1, 2010, in Richmond, VA, I say:

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-052 Position
positions.thesis: * "On July 1, 2010, Jim is 3,000 miles from Richmond VA." The information encoded in (i) doesn't follow from the information encoded in (ii), or vice versa. In general, when the context-sensitive expressions in a true statement are replaced with context-insensitive expressions, the result is a statement that, although true, neither entails the first statement not follows from it. For an expression to be context-sensitive is for its referent to vary with the context. What an expression refers to is determined by its semantics, not its syntax. CTM says that thought is to no degree semantics-driven. If that were true, we couldn't grasp perspectival truths, such as that expressed by (i). Since we can grasp such truths, CTM is false. 5.0 CTM’s reliance on the erroneous assumption that there are minimal units of thought First a definition: a “morpheme” is an expression that cannot be decomposed into others. Bearing this in mind, let’s suppose that, just as CTM says, we think in a language. In that case, our concepts are identical with expressions. Linguistic expressions consist of morphemes. (Any given complex expression; e.g., “the cat is on the mat,” decomposes into expressions that cannot, in their turn, be further decomposed; (e.g., “cat,” “mat”). So in contending that our thoughts are identical with (tokens of) Mentalese symbols, CTM is, in effect, assuming that we have simple concepts: concepts that cannot be decomposed in others. So CTM, if correct, entails that any given thought that one has can be decomposed into “simple” concepts. CTM thus has the radically implausible consequence that we have “simple” concepts. In other words, CTM has the consequence that some of our concepts are no more capable of being decomposed into other concepts than a morpheme (e.g., the word “cat”) can be decomposed into other expressions. If this consequence of CTM is correct, it follows, as we’ll now see, that some of our concepts would be such that one could have them without also having other concepts. Let T be the token of the Mentalese word for “snow” that went off when, just a moment ago, I thought to myself that snow is white. We may assume that T is a morpheme. (If, for some reason, you reject that assumption, just map what we say about T onto some Mentalese expression-token that, in your judgment, is a morpheme—and you must, if you grant that we think in...

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-053 Position
positions.thesis: * Some of our concepts are such that one can have themwithout having others. This means that, if CTM is right, your concept of snow is primitive, the same being true of your concept of Smith, Jones, and Brown (assuming, for argument’s sake, that the Mentalese expressions for these individuals are morphemic). 5.1 CTM’s reliance on the erroneous assumption that there are minimal units of thought (continued) But there are no primitive concepts. I couldn’t possibly have a concept of snow, or of Barack Obama, or (to take a more tendentious example) of the number three without having a network of other concepts---extension, spatiotemporal location, surface-area, sentience, etc. Concepts are necessarily contrastive; in order to have a concept of a thing, one has to be able to distinguish that thing from other things. A prerequisite for distinguishing a given rock from a given tree is my being aware of some property had by the rock that isn’t had by the tree. And a prerequisite for my having such knowledge is my already having concepts. 5.1.1 How some contemporary philosophers would response to this argument Many contemporary theorists---some of them CTM-advocates, some of them not—reject this line of thought, saying that, to have a “fix,” on the rock (or the tree), I need only be on the receiving end of a causal series beginning with it. Their view is that concepts are mental photographs. The relationship borne by my concept of Socrates with respect to Socrates himself is identical with the relationship borne by a photograph of Barack Obama with respect to the events (the impact of certain light-rays with Barack Obama’s body) that, in due course, led to that photograph. The position just described involves a failure to distinguish between:

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-054 Position
positions.thesis: * information that, if known, would lead to such awareness. I see discolorations of a certain kind on a photographic plate. Given that information, along with other pieces of background information that I have, I can make various inferences. I can infer that, at some point, light bounced off of a person who was bald and was wearing a toga (and so on). So the photographic plate is information in the sense that, to a creature with the requisite background and the requisite intelligence, its existence warrants the making of an inference that will eventuate in knowledge of some entity (e.g., a person) or state of affairs (e.g., one consisting of a person wearing a toga) that might otherwise have remained unknown. (Why “might otherwise have been unknown,” instead of “would otherwise have been unknown”? Because I might have been the one who took the picture and might therefore have already known of the person and the corresponding state of affairs.) The discolorations on the photographic plate are “information” in the sense that, to a suitably endowed creature, they warrant the making of inferences that, in due course, lead to concepts. In any case, it is massively to CTM’s discredit that it presupposes that there are representational atoms. 5.2 A similar argument Sentences are digital structures. Setting aside cases of ambiguity, any given sentence—any given complex expression, in fact---has a unique decomposition into minimal units of significance. This means, as previously pointed out, that our thoughts decompose into minimal representational units. Which is radically implausible. In any case, sense-perceptions don’t have a digital structure, and neither, it would seem to follow, does the information that sense-perceptions encode. Given which, it would be very artificial to maintain that all post-perceptual cognition was digital or, therefore, sentential in nature. But, for argument’s sake, let’s suppose that the contents of our senseperceptions can, as some advocates of CTM maintain, be “digitized.” Supposing this hypothesis correct, all the problems previously posed for CTM by perceptual content are now posed by the post-perceptual thoughts involved in this (alleged) digitization-process. If I’m translating from Albanian to English, I must speak both Albanian and English. Moreover, supposing that I’m translating competently, I have some way of evaluating the degree to which my English outputs match my Albanian inputs. If my thinking is entirely mediated by English sentences, I won’t be able to do either of these things:...

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-055 Position
positions.thesis: * “All non-black things are non-ravens.” The existence of non-ravens (e.g., pianos, pants, carpet fibers) that are pink or green confirms (2), but does not, unless our intuitions are very wrong, confirm (1). In any case, discovery of a black raven does more in the way of confirming (1) than does discovery of a pink piano, and discovery of a pink piano does more in the way of confirming (1) than does discovery of a black raven. So, impossible though it must be, (1) and (2) are logically, but not confirmationally, equivalent. An analogous paradox is associated with each one of infinitely many other pairs of logically equivalent sentences, many of them of scientifically important. For example:

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-056 Position
positions.thesis: * “if it isn’t expanding, then it isn’t both made of metal and beingheated.” (ii) is confirmed by the fact that the non-expanding Tupperware in my freezer is not made of metal (or a fortiori of metal that is being heated). But, unless our intuitions are very wrong, that fact provides no support for (i), or at least provides less support for it than it does for (ii). Some, including Hempel himself, have argued that, our intuitions notwithstanding, the existence of pink pianos does confirm (1) to degree n exactly if it confirms (2) to that same degree and that the existence of nonexpanding Tupperware in freezers does confirm (i) exactly as much as it [342] confirms (ii). Others (e.g. Quine ) have argued that the paradox should be solved by advocating non-standard logics, relative to which logical [343] equivalence doesn’t guarantee confirmational equivalence. Whatever merits these solutions have, it’s worth trying to solve Hempel’s paradox in a manner congenial to commonsense-friendly and also with classical (meta)logical principles. And we’ll now see that such a solution is available. [344] The trick is to look at what (i) and (ii) convey, and not just at what they can (reasonably) be taken to have for their literal meanings. The trick, in other words, is too look, not just at semantics, but also at pragmatics. Bearing this in mind, let us now (in Section 1.1) discuss an assumption we’ll make [345] concerning the semantics of categoricals. 2.0 Inductions by enumeration identical with crypto-inferences to the best explanation In this context, (i) may be treated as saying the same thing as: (MH) For any x, if x is made of metal and x is being heated, then x is expanding. And (ii) may be treated as saying the same thing as: (HM) For any x, if x isn’t expanding, then x isn’t made of metal that is being heated. MH can be taken either to express an “accidental generalization” or to express a principled relationship. Taken in the first way, it does nothing more than affirm a purely de facto concomitance of two properties (viz. the property of being heated metal and the property of being something that expands). Taken in the second way, MH seems to say that all heated metal expands, the reason being that a thing’s being heated metal provides some kind of nomic basis for its expanding. In this section,...

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-057 Position
positions.thesis: * “If all F’s are G’s, and no G’s are H’s, then no F’s are H’s.”(4) “Either Helsinki is in Finland or Helsinki is not in Finland.” [354] What is “formal truth”? What does it mean to describe (1), for example, as “formally true”? Here’s the canonical answer: (FT) Formal truth is syntactic truth. A sentence is “syntactically true” if, given only its syntactic structure, it cannot fail to be true. Thus, a sentence is formally true if no sentence having the same syntactic structure is false. FT cannot be accepted. A linguist would say that (1) “grass is green or grass is not green” has the same syntax as (2) “grass is green and grass is not green.” As linguists use the word “syntax,” two expressions have the same syntax if the way in which the one is built out of its parts is identical with the way in which the other is built out of its parts. For example, (#) “Bill hates Ted” and (##) “Laura loves Sally,” though having very different meanings, have the same syntax, the reason being that they’re structurally identical. The one is built out of its simple

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-058 Position
positions.thesis: * constituents in the very way that the other is built out of its constituents. The relevant difference is substructural. It lies in the fact that the meaning of “and” is different from that of “or.” So the difference between (1) and (2) is like the difference between “John is tall” and “Mary is short”: it lies entirely in the fact that the primitive, semantically unstructured constituents of the one have different meanings from their counterparts in the other. Thus, relative to what linguists mean by “syntax” (and “syntactic,” etc.), it’s a non-starter to say that “formal truth” is syntactic truth. Logicians and philosophers obviously have in mind some very different understanding of the word “syntax” when they identify “formal” truth with “syntactic” truth. They don’t have in mind sentential (or phrasal) structure. What do they have in mind? What do philosophers mean when they say that a formally true sentence is one that holds entirely in virtue of its “syntax”? What do they mean when they describe some statement as a “formal truth”? 2.0 Quine on formal truth: logical/formal truth as truth under all reinterpretations of non-logical expressions W.V.O. Quine (1970) gave a characteristically intelligent and straightforward answer to this important question: a sentence S is formally true if, given any expression in S that isn’t a logical constant, it isn’t possible to produce a false sentence by replacing that expression with another expression of the appropriate grammatical

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-059 Position
positions.thesis: * category. A “logical constant” is an expression on the following list: “if . . . then . . . ,” “either . . . or . . . ,” “not,” “for all,” “for some,” “=”—and any obvious derivatives of any of these expressions (e.g., “is identical with,” [357] “given any,” “it is not the case that,” etc.) Consider the sentence “grass is green or grass is not green.” Replacing “grass” with “fire” or “Bob” results in a truth, as does replacing “green” with “cool” or “voluminous.” (It’s assumed that the replacements are uniformly made: if a single occurrence of “grass” is replaced with “fire,” so is every other such occurrence.) Given any expression in (1) other than the logical constants in it (viz., “either . . . or . . .” and “not”), replacing that expression with another belonging to the same grammatical category results in truth. There are two problems with Quine’s analysis. First, it doesn’t say what is so special about those items; it doesn’t say what these so-called “logical constants” have in common with one another that other expressions not on the list don’t have in common with them. And until that information is provided, there’s nothing to prevent one from extending that list in arbitrary ways. Unless that list was generated by some rule, anything can be added to it without diminishing its integrity, since it has no integrity to begin with. But if it was generated by some rule, then Quine’s answer fails to identify it and thus fails to say what formal truth is. An analogy may help. When asked to define the expression “number,” I simply list the various different kinds of numbers (“real,” “whole,” “rational,” “imaginary,” etc.). Have I answered the question? No. Even though I’ve listed the various different kinds of numbers, I haven’t said what numbers are, and I thus haven’t provided any reason why fractions are on that list but birds are not. Accordingly, my answer doesn’t give anyone a reason not to extend that list as he or she pleases—not to decide that birds and rocks are numbers. Of course, nobody would extend the list in such an absurd way. But that’s only because our intuitions strongly suggest that there is some characteristic had in common by reals, rationals, etc., that birds and rocks don’t have. Second, there are truths not covered by Quine’s analysis that don’t...

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-060 Position
positions.thesis: * For any propositions P, Q, and R, ‹if P and Q and R, then [359] R › is true. But, (A) is not itself a formal truth. (A) is true, of course. But it is not in virtue of its being an instance of a universally true open-sentence that it’s true. For it isn’t an instance of such a sentence. Any open-sentence of which (A) is an instance is one of which:

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-061 Position
positions.thesis: * For no propositions P, Q, and R, ‹if P and Q and R, then R› is true, which is obviously false (and thus not true, formally or otherwise). Since (B) is an instance of any open-sentence of which (A) is an instance, the former is not formally true. It is in virtue not only of its form, but also of what is meant by “for any propositions P, Q, and R” that (A) is true. (A) and (B) have the same form. What distinguishes them is that, in the place where (B) contains an occurrence of one expression (“for no propositions P, Q, and R”), (A) contains an expression with a different meaning (“for any propositions P, Q, and R”). So the fact that A is analytic, whereas B is not, cannot possibly be chalked up to the fact that one is formally true, whereas the other is not. Thus, not all analytic truth is formal truth. In the TLP, Wittgenstein[360] says that all analytic truth is formal truth. Wittgenstein is wrong, for the reasons just given. Also, even if there were some formally true sentence S such that S was equivalent with (A), the statement (in this context you make take "statement" to mean "sentence,” "sentence-token,” or "proposition"): (C) S is equivalent with A. would not itself be a formal truth. To be a formal truth is to be a consequence of an informally true universal generalization. The sentence: (%) if grass is green and snow is white and roses are red, then roses are red is a formal truth because it is a consequence of (A). Thus, Wittgenstein’s thesis that all analytic truth is formal truth is not only wrong but incoherent. Here is another illustration of that fact. The sentence: (JTB) any case of knowledge is a case of justified true belief, is an analytic, not an empirical, truth. But it isn’t a formal truth, since it has the same form as: (NTB) any case of knowledge is a case of unjustified false belief, which is false. Wittgenstein grants that there seem to be analytically true sentences that aren’t formally true. But he denies that there really are such sentences. His position is that, although it seems not to be formally true, the sentence: (JTB) any case of knowledge is a case of justified true belief, is in fact formally true, the reason being that it...

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-062 Position
positions.thesis: * “it’s formally true that ‘snow is white only if snow is whiteor grass is green’ is formally true.” It’s an empirical truth, not a truth of logic, that (i) is even an expression, let alone a true, or a fortiori formally, true one. To be sure, it is not an empirical truth that, the semantic and syntactic rules of English being what they are, (iii) is true. In other words,

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-063 Position
positions.thesis: * Assuming that S1…Sn are the semantic/syntactic rules of English, it (logically) follows that is that (i) is formally true. But (iii) is not itself a formal truth. But there’s no denying that it’s a truth of logic. But for (iii), or some other truth of its ilk (viz. some non-empirical, informal truth), there’s no way to establish that (i) is formally true. In general, informal truths are needed to establish whether a given sentence is formally true or not. It’s only on the basis of informal truths that it can be determined whether a given truth is formal or not. In other words, if formal truth is logical truth, then it’s only on the basis of non-logical truths that it can be determined whether a given statement is formally true or not---a statement’s being logically true would be a non-logical consequence of some non-logical truth. But in that case, the term “logic” wouldn’t refer to the body of truths with respect to which the legitimacy of an inference or argument is to be evaluated; it would refer to specific applications of those truths. It would refer to the consequences of contingent truths. More precisely, it would refer to truths, e.g. the consequent of (iii), that follow from truths, e.g. the antecedent of (iii) that describe linguistic conventions that happen to hold but needn’t do so. Be it noted that, although the consequent of (iii) follows necessarily from the antecedent, the antecedent is contingent and so is the consequent. It is a contingent fact, as we’ve noted, that (for any appropriate value of P) “if P, then not-not P” is an expression, let alone a true one, let alone a formally true one. And it’s a contingent fact that E1…En, these being the rules constitutive of the English language, are in fact operative. So if logical truth coincided with formal truth, the truths recognized and operated on by those gifted with logical acumen would in all cases be contingent. But logic is the study of necessary truth (or, in any case, of a certain kind of necessary truth), and logical acumen is therefore the ability to identify and delineate the consequences of such truths. 4.2 The doubly relative nature of formal truth Consider the sentence: (a) “Jim is tall iff Jim is tall”

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-064 Position
positions.thesis: * Jim is tall or Jim is tall, is not formally true. (b) is equivalent with “Jim is tall” and (if true) is therefore empirically true. Any instance of “P iff P” is formally true since all its instances are true. [361] But not all instances of “P or P” are formally true, since some of its instances are false, e.g. “Jim is a square circle or Jim is a square circle”. (b) is either contingently true or contingently false, since some of its instances are true (e.g. “Paris in France or Paris is in France”) and some are not (e.g. “Paris in Germany or Paris is in Germany”). But [362] (AT 1) “all statements of the form P iff P are formally true” is not itself a formal truth. After all, it has the same form as: (TA1) “all statements of the form P iff P are empirically true,” which is false. Given any formal truth E belonging to any language L, the proposition it is formally true that E is formally true is false. “Either it’s raining here or it’s not raining here” is formally true. But [363] (IR) “the sentence ‘either it’s raining here or it’s not raining here’ is formally true,” though true, is not formally true. It has the same form as: (RI) “the sentence ‘either it’s raining here or it’s not raining here’ is obviously false,” which is false. It is always, in the final analysis, on the basis of knowledge of non-formal truths that we know a given truth to be formal or that we know all statements of a certain form to be true. It’s an empirical fact that “and,” “iff,” “or,” and “because” have the syntactic and semantic properties that they do. But it’s non-empirically true that, given that they have those properties, the first three are truth-functional and the fourth is not. That truth is analytic but nonformal. And so is the truth that, the relevant syntactic rules being what they are, “P iff P” is always true, “P or P” and “P and P” are sometimes true and sometimes false, and “P because P” is (I should think) always false. There is no way to formalize the insights on the basis of which we know that “P iff P” (“P and P”) is always (sometimes). Given that knowledge, one can sometimes determine whether a given sentence is true or...

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-065 Position
positions.thesis: * x is phi. (1) is a true sentence. (2)–(4) are neither true nor false. Given (1), (2) is what results when “Bill Gates” is replaced with a variable, (3) is what results when “wealthy” is replaced with a variable, and (4) is what results when both are replaced with variables. (See Chapter 7.) Of course, actual sentences are formed out of these open-sentences by replacing the variables in (2)-(4) with constants. In each case, this procedure may yield either a true sentence or a false one. For example, replacing the variable in (2) with “Bill Gates” yields a truth and replacing it with “Mike Smith,” the name of an impecunious friend of mine, yields a falsehood. Bearing these points in mind, consider the following expressions:

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-066 Position
positions.thesis: * UG) For any individual x and any property phi, if x is phi, then it’s not the case that x is not phi. (9UG) For any proposition P, if P, then it’s not the case that not-P. This suggests that a sentence is formally true if it’s an instance of a universally true open-sentence. To take another example:

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-067 Position
positions.thesis: * If it’s necessarily the case that Smith is a mammal ifSmith is a dog, then it’s necessarily the case that Smith is a mammal if it’s necessarily the case that Smith is a dog; is formally true, because it’s an instance of the following universally correct open-sentences:

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-068 Position
positions.thesis: * If it’s necessarily the case that Q if P, then it’s necessarily the case that Q if it’s necessarily the case that P. 5.1 What is a logical constant? Quine’s analysis, we noted, is vitiated by his failing to say what conditions an expression must satisfy to be a “logical constant.” We are in position to provide this information. The expressions “not” and “if . . . then . . .” are on Quine’s list of logical constants, and those expressions occur in: (5) If Smith is at home, then it’s not the case that Smith is not at home. Before we proceed, we have to make a couple of points about the word “not.” That word not is known as a “sentence-level operator”: it’s an expression that forms sentences out of sentences—it forms “snow is not white” out of “snow is white.” It isn’t often that sentence-level operators are replaced with variables. But not only can such replacements be made; they must be made. Otherwise, it would be impossible to express any generalization about sentence-level connectives. (Logicians and linguists often make statements about whole classes of sentence-level connectives, and such statements involve replacements of this kind, since they have the form: “given any sentence level connective X . . .”) In fact, we’re about to state just such a principle right now. Given (5), if we replace “not” with a variable, the result is: (5 X) If Smith is at home, then it’s X the case that Smith is X at home. If we bind the free variable with a universal quantifier of the appropriate kind, the result is: (5 XUG) Given any sentence-level operator X, if Smith is at home, then it’s X the case that Smith is X at home, which is false, the reason being that some sentence-level operators lack the relevant property. An example of such an operator is the word “necessarily.” When this word replaces the variable in (5X), the result is: (5 XN) If Smith is at home, then it’s necessarily the case that Smith is necessarily at home, which is false. Another expression on Quine’s list that occurs in (5) is “if . . . then . . .” This expression, like “not,” is a sentence-level operator. But whereas “not” is a one-placed sentence-connective, “if . . . then . . .” is a two-place sentence connective. So, whereas “not” assigns...

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-069 Position
positions.thesis: * One can travel backwards in time. It is generally thought that (a)–(c) are empirical questions that fall within the jurisdiction of physics. In actuality, they are questions of logic, not of empirical fact. Moreover, each of (a)–(c) is logically false and therefore [368] couldn’t possibly be correct. Let’s discuss (a) first. In this chapter, we have seen that anything that persists for any amount of time is a causal series. A causal series is a continuous series of states of affairs such that, given any two of them, one is the (in)direct cause of the other. An illustration may be helpful. At any given moment during its existence, your car consists of an incessant flurry of mass-energy displacements; and your car is thus a series of “event-flurries,” as we’ll call them. Twenty-four hours ago, your car was constituted by one event-flurry. Right now it’s constituted by a different event-flurry. (Let S1 be the first event-flurry and let S2 be the second.) And, of course, my car is currently constituted by some event-flurry. (Let S3 be that flurry.) Bearing all of this in mind, imagine the following. While your car was parked in your driveway, somebody crashed into it, severely damaging it. Your car therefore bears little resemblance to what it was twenty-four hours ago. It follows that S1 and S2 are qualitatively very different from each other. By contrast, S1 and S3 are as similar to each other as their being in different places permits. This is sheer coincidence: S1 and S3 are, we will suppose, as causally disconnected from each other as any two non-simultaneous events on this planet can be. It follows that my car, as it is right now, is just like your car, as it was twenty-four hours ago, before it was damaged. It also follows that S1 is much less similar to S2 than it is to S3. Be all of this as it may, the car in your driveway is, whereas the car in my driveway is not, numerically identical with the car you owned twenty-four hours ago. Mediating between S1 and S2 is a continuous series of events such that, given any two of them, one is the (in)direct cause of the other. No such series mediates between S1 and S3. That’s why (i) the car in my driveway isn’t the very same car as the one you parked in your driveway...

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-070 Position
positions.thesis: * Smith is annihilated in 2009 and a Smith-like entity that isn’t actually Smith ex nihilo comes into existence in the year 2023. A question will help clarify the reasoning behind this last assertion: Where is Smith during that fourteen year interval? He’s either nowhere or he’s somewhere. Let’s suppose that he’s nowhere. In that case, for the reasons illustrated in connection with your car, whatever it is that pops into existence in the year 2023 isn’t numerically identical with Smith. So let’s suppose that he’s somewhere— that, at any given instant during that time period, he is in some place or other. But in that case he’s no more time-traveling than anything else that exists during that interval. If he’s next to some one tree during that entire period, he’s no more time-traveling than that tree. If he’s going from place to place, he’s no more time-traveling than any one of the satellites that will orbit the [369] earth during that period. So depending on whether he’s somewhere or nowhere, Smith is either permanently annihilated in 2009 or he’s no more time-traveling than anything else that exists during that period. Therefore, (i) is false. To see why (ii) is false we need do little more than repeat some of the points just made. Suppose that Smith travels from 2009 to 2023 at a rate of one million minutes/minute. We’ve just seen that Smith cannot make this journey in a discontinuous manner; he cannot disappear from one time and reappear at some other, non-adjacent time. So Smith must make this journey in a continuous manner. So at any given moment during this interval, Smith must be in some place or other. It follows, as we saw a moment ago, that he is no more time-traveling than anything else that exists during that interval. The supposition that Smith is traveling forward in time at a rate of one million minutes/minute thus turns out to collapse into the supposition that he isn’t time-traveling at all. Given any positive number n, an analogous argument shows that continuous forward time-travel at a rate of n minutes/minute collapses into forward time-travel at a rate of one minute/minute. There is another, quite different problem with the concept of discontinuous time-travel. Let W1 be a universe where (i) is true and (ii) is false; and let W2 be an otherwise indistinguishable universe where (ii) is true and...

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-071 Position
positions.thesis: * Marty McFly was annihilated in 1985, and in 1955 thereappears ex nihilo somebody who is numerically distinct from him but otherwise just like him. Also, given obvious adaptations of what we said a moment ago, there is no conceivable event that, were it known, would provide more support for (1) than for (2). Since, therefore, there couldn’t possibly be any observation-based reason for preferring (1) to (2), the only possible reasons for doing so would be of a logical nature. This means that there are no reasons for doing so, given that (1) is incoherent. The concept of discontinuous backwards time-travel is therefore a broken one. What about the idea of continuous backwards time-travel? For argument’s sake, let’s suppose that, during his continuous journey from the year 1985 to the year 1955, Marty McFly is right next to some stop-sign that was built in 1920 and stays in the same place for several centuries. That means that, at any given moment during that thirty year period, Marty McFly is no more time-traveling than the stop-sign. Each is just sitting there. There are, of course, some important differences between Mary McFly and the stop-sign. First of all, whereas the stop-sign is crushed or burnt or otherwise destroyed in some pedestrian way, Marty McFly must simply vanish in the year 1985. (If he doesn’t, then he’s still hanging around in the year 1985, in which case he obviously hasn’t gone back in time.) And, whereas the stop-sign’s presence in the year 1955 is to be explained in the customary way, Marty McFly just popped up ex nihilo. Bearing these points in mind, let W3 be a universe in which: (3) Marty McFly continuously traveled backwards in time from the year 1985 to the year 1955 but is otherwise unchanged. is true, and in which (2) is false; and let W4 be a universe that is exactly like W3 except that, in it, (2) is true and (3) is false. Echoing what we said earlier, there is no conceivable event that, were it known, would enable one to determine whether one was in W3 as opposed to W4, or vice versa. Which, taken together with the fact that being annihilated in 1985 didn’t exactly tighten young Marty’s grip on existence, suggests that, other things being equal, (2) is the better way to model the observational data. There is another, even deeper issue....

positions.source: Conception and Causation: Selected Early Philosophical Papers
positions.title: CONCAUS-072 Position
positions.thesis: * there is a justification (a reason) for the occurrence of any given event. Be it noted that it is events (or conditions) that have causes, whereas it is propositions that have justifications. Whether (i) is true is not the concern of this paper. Until I recently heard a lecture by Paul Humphries, I accepted (ii). I now reject, on the basis (partly) [373] of what Humphries said. What I wll say, though in some m ways a development of what Humphreys said, is not Humphries-exegesis, incorporating into it large elements of my own reactions to his talk. Humphreys’ denial of (ii) consisted in his advocating the contention that: (H) a given event E1 can cause (or the affirmation of its existence can justify) the occurrence (or affirmation thereof) of an event E2, even if, given E1’s occurrence, there was only an n% chance (where n is any number greater than 1 and less than 100) of E2’s occurrence. I believe H correct and would like to defend it, borrowing heavily (to say the least) in my attempt to do so from Humphries’ argument. A preliminary argument. Complying with laws is different from being governed by them. Everything complies with the laws of microphysics; and, presumably, all higher level laws (assuming such thing to exist, which I will argue they do) they hold in virtue of microphysical laws. But consider this. We have two houses that are architecturally identical, meaning that they way in which their medium sized parts (their constituent bricks, planks, etc.) are interrelated are identical.
